short_description: Left Front Wheel Speed Sensor
detailed_technical_explanation: Code C0031 (Left Front Wheel Speed Sensor) sets when the ABS module loses a valid electrical signal from a wheel speed sensor -- the circuit reads open, shorted low or high, drops out intermittently, fails to correlate with the other three wheels, or reports an incorrect component installed. The diagnosis and repair are identical at every corner: a corroded or unplugged connector, a chafed harness conductor rubbing on the strut or subframe, or a failed Hall-effect/variable-reluctance sensor element. This is distinct from a tone-ring or mechanical fault, where the wiring and sensor test fine but the signal is noisy or absent because of physical damage at the reluctor wheel.
fix_title: Inspect wheel speed sensor connector and harness routing
step_by_step_instructions: Locate the sensor connector near the hub or knuckle at the affected corner • Unplug and inspect for corrosion, moisture, or a bent pin • Follow the harness up to the body grommet checking for chafing against the strut or subframe • Clean, reseat, clear the code, and road test

short_description: Left Front Wheel Speed Sensor
detailed_technical_explanation: Code C0031 (Left Front Wheel Speed Sensor) sets when the ABS module loses a valid electrical signal from a wheel speed sensor -- the circuit reads open, shorted low or high, drops out intermittently, fails to correlate with the other three wheels, or reports an incorrect component installed. The diagnosis and repair are identical at every corner: a corroded or unplugged connector, a chafed harness conductor rubbing on the strut or subframe, or a failed Hall-effect/variable-reluctance sensor element. This is distinct from a tone-ring or mechanical fault, where the wiring and sensor test fine but the signal is noisy or absent because of physical damage at the reluctor wheel.
fix_title: Test wheel speed sensor resistance and signal
step_by_step_instructions: Unplug the sensor and measure coil resistance against the factory specification • Reconnect and back-probe the signal circuit while rotating the wheel by hand • Confirm a clean, consistent AC signal with no dropouts • Replace the sensor if resistance is out of range or the signal is erratic

short_description: Left Front Wheel Speed Sensor
detailed_technical_explanation: Code C0031 (Left Front Wheel Speed Sensor) sets when the ABS module loses a valid electrical signal from a wheel speed sensor -- the circuit reads open, shorted low or high, drops out intermittently, fails to correlate with the other three wheels, or reports an incorrect component installed. This is distinct from a tone-ring or mechanical fault, where the wiring and sensor test fine but the signal is noisy or absent because of physical damage at the reluctor wheel.
fix_title: Replace wheel speed sensor
step_by_step_instructions: Disconnect the battery negative terminal • Remove the sensor mounting bolt and pull the sensor from the knuckle • Install the replacement sensor, confirming it seats fully against the tone ring • Clear the code and confirm signal quality across a range of speeds

short_description: Left Front Wheel Speed Sensor
detailed_technical_explanation: Code C0031 (Left Front Wheel Speed Sensor) sets when the ABS module loses a valid electrical signal from a wheel speed sensor -- the circuit reads open, shorted low or high, drops out intermittently, fails to correlate with the other three wheels, or reports an incorrect component installed. The diagnosis and repair are identical at every corner: a corroded or unplugged connector, a chafed harness conductor rubbing on the strut or subframe, or a failed Hall-effect/variable-reluctance sensor element. This is distinct from a tone-ring or mechanical fault, where the wiring and sensor test fine but the signal is noisy or absent because of physical damage at the reluctor wheel.
fix_title: Repair chafed wheel speed sensor harness wiring
step_by_step_instructions: Trace the harness from the sensor to the body connector along the strut and control arm • Identify the chafe point, commonly where the harness crosses suspension travel • Repair damaged conductors with solder and heat-shrink, then re-route and secure the harness • Clear codes and confirm all four corners correlate correctly on a road test

short_description: Brake Pedal Switch A
detailed_technical_explanation: Code C0040 (Brake Pedal Switch A) sets when the module sees an implausible state from the brake pedal switch(es) that both illuminate the stop lamps and tell the ABS/cruise/shift-interlock systems the brake pedal is applied -- the switch stays closed, stays open, or the two redundant switch channels disagree with each other. A misadjusted switch plunger, a worn switch contact, or corrosion at the switch connector near the pedal bracket are the common causes. Because this switch also feeds the stop lamps and brake-transmission shift interlock, a fault here can cause symptoms well beyond ABS, from stuck-on brake lights to a shifter that will not release from Park.
fix_title: Adjust brake pedal switch plunger clearance
step_by_step_instructions: Locate the brake pedal switch mounted on the pedal bracket above the pedal arm • Loosen the switch locknut and adjust plunger contact per the service manual clearance spec • Pull the pedal fully rearward to auto-adjust the switch on platforms that support it • Verify stop lamps and scan tool brake switch PID respond correctly to pedal travel

short_description: Brake Pedal Switch A
detailed_technical_explanation: Code C0040 (Brake Pedal Switch A) sets when the module sees an implausible state from the brake pedal switch(es) that both illuminate the stop lamps and tell the ABS/cruise/shift-interlock systems the brake pedal is applied -- the switch stays closed, stays open, or the two redundant switch channels disagree with each other. A misadjusted switch plunger, a worn switch contact, or corrosion at the switch connector near the pedal bracket are the common causes. Because this switch also feeds the stop lamps and brake-transmission shift interlock, a fault here can cause symptoms well beyond ABS, from stuck-on brake lights to a shifter that will not release from Park.
fix_title: Inspect brake pedal switch connector
step_by_step_instructions: Unplug the switch connector and inspect pins for corrosion • Clean with electrical contact cleaner and reseat the connector • Confirm both switch channels toggle together with a multimeter • Clear the code and verify stop lamp operation

short_description: Brake Pedal Switch A
detailed_technical_explanation: Code C0040 (Brake Pedal Switch A) sets when the module sees an implausible state from the brake pedal switch(es) that both illuminate the stop lamps and tell the ABS/cruise/shift-interlock systems the brake pedal is applied -- the switch stays closed, stays open, or the two redundant switch channels disagree with each other. fix_title: Replace brake pedal switch
step_by_step_instructions: Disconnect the battery negative terminal • Rotate and remove the switch from the pedal bracket • Install the replacement switch and adjust plunger clearance to spec • Clear codes and confirm correct stop lamp and brake-applied signal operation

short_description: Brake Pedal Switch A
detailed_technical_explanation: Code C0040 (Brake Pedal Switch A) sets when the module sees an implausible state from the brake pedal switch(es) that both illuminate the stop lamps and tell the ABS/cruise/shift-interlock systems the brake pedal is applied -- the switch stays closed, stays open, or the two redundant switch channels disagree with each other. fix_title: Repair stop lamp/brake switch circuit wiring
step_by_step_instructions: Trace the harness from the switch to the module and stop lamp fuse • Check for chafing near the pedal bracket and steering column • Repair with solder and heat-shrink where damaged • Verify both switch channels and stop lamps function correctly

short_description: A Camshaft Position - Timing Over-Advanced or System Performance Bank 1
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0011 (A Camshaft Position - Timing Over-Advanced or System Performance Bank 1) sets when a camshaft phaser reaches a timing position beyond commanded limits or becomes stuck rather than responding to duty-cycle commands. Root causes are sludge or low oil pressure starving the phaser, a worn phaser with excessive internal slack, a stuck oil control valve, or a mis-timed chain/belt from prior service.
fix_title: Change engine oil and filter to spec viscosity
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Drain and inspect oil condition for sludge or debris • 2. Replace with OEM-viscosity oil and a new filter • 3. Clear codes and run a phaser sweep test with a scan tool • 4. Road test through varied load to confirm stable timing

short_description: A Camshaft Position - Timing Over-Advanced or System Performance Bank 1
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0011 (A Camshaft Position - Timing Over-Advanced or System Performance Bank 1) sets when a camshaft phaser reaches a timing position beyond commanded limits or becomes stuck rather than responding to duty-cycle commands. Root causes are sludge or low oil pressure starving the phaser, a worn phaser with excessive internal slack, a stuck oil control valve, or a mis-timed chain/belt from prior service.
fix_title: Replace oil control valve and clean phaser feed passage
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Remove the OCV solenoid and inspect its filter screen • 2. Clean the oil feed passage to the phaser of sludge with solvent and compressed air • 3. Install a new OCV solenoid • 4. Clear adaptive learning and verify timing response

short_description: A Camshaft Position - Timing Over-Advanced or System Performance Bank 1
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0011 (A Camshaft Position - Timing Over-Advanced or System Performance Bank 1) sets when a camshaft phaser reaches a timing position beyond commanded limits or becomes stuck rather than responding to duty-cycle commands. Root causes are sludge or low oil pressure starving the phaser, a worn phaser with excessive internal slack, a stuck oil control valve, or a mis-timed chain/belt from prior service.
fix_title: Replace worn camshaft phaser assembly
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Set the engine to TDC and lock the timing chain • 2. Remove the worn phaser and inspect the chain/tensioner for related wear • 3. Install the new phaser and retime to factory marks • 4. Verify actuator sweep and clear codes

short_description: A Camshaft Position - Timing Over-Advanced or System Performance Bank 1
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0011 (A Camshaft Position - Timing Over-Advanced or System Performance Bank 1) sets when a camshaft phaser reaches a timing position beyond commanded limits or becomes stuck rather than responding to duty-cycle commands. Root causes are sludge or low oil pressure starving the phaser, a worn phaser with excessive internal slack, a stuck oil control valve, or a mis-timed chain/belt from prior service.
fix_title: Correct timing chain/belt installation
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Remove the timing cover and verify marks against factory specification • 2. Correct any tooth-count misalignment on the chain or belt • 3. Replace the tensioner and guides while accessed • 4. Confirm cam/crank correlation and phaser response

short_description: Crankshaft Position - Camshaft Position Correlation Bank 1 Sensor A
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0016 (Crankshaft Position - Camshaft Position Correlation Bank 1 Sensor A) sets when the ECM compares crankshaft and camshaft position signals and finds their relative timing outside the expected window, or cannot establish a stable engine position sync. This is most often caused by timing chain/belt stretch or a jumped tooth, a failing crankshaft or camshaft position sensor, worn tensioner/guides, or reluctor-wheel damage.
fix_title: Inspect and replace crankshaft/camshaft position sensor
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Identify which sensor the correlation fault points to on the scan tool freeze frame • 2. Unplug and remove the affected position sensor • 3. Inspect the reluctor wheel/tone ring for chips or debris • 4. Install the new sensor with correct air-gap shim and clear codes

short_description: Crankshaft Position - Camshaft Position Correlation Bank 1 Sensor A
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0016 (Crankshaft Position - Camshaft Position Correlation Bank 1 Sensor A) sets when the ECM compares crankshaft and camshaft position signals and finds their relative timing outside the expected window, or cannot establish a stable engine position sync. This is most often caused by timing chain/belt stretch or a jumped tooth, a failing crankshaft or camshaft position sensor, worn tensioner/guides, or reluctor-wheel damage.
fix_title: Inspect timing chain/belt for stretch or jumped timing
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Remove the timing cover and verify cam-to-crank alignment against factory timing marks • 2. Measure chain stretch or check belt tooth wear • 3. Replace the timing chain/belt, tensioner, and guides as a set • 4. Reset timing to factory marks and verify correlation with a scan tool

short_description: Crankshaft Position - Camshaft Position Correlation Bank 1 Sensor A
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0016 (Crankshaft Position - Camshaft Position Correlation Bank 1 Sensor A) sets when the ECM compares crankshaft and camshaft position signals and finds their relative timing outside the expected window, or cannot establish a stable engine position sync. This is most often caused by timing chain/belt stretch or a jumped tooth, a failing crankshaft or camshaft position sensor, worn tensioner/guides, or reluctor-wheel damage.
fix_title: Repair crank/cam sensor wiring and connectors
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Back-probe the sensor signal and ground at each connector while cranking • 2. Voltage-drop test the harness runs back to the ECM • 3. Repair chafed conductors near the timing cover with heat-shrink splices • 4. Confirm clean square-wave signals on a scope

short_description: Mass or Volume Air Flow Sensor A Circuit Range/Performance
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0101 (Mass or Volume Air Flow Sensor A Circuit Range/Performance) sets when the ECM detects an implausible signal from the mass air flow sensor — reported airflow disagrees with throttle position and engine speed, or the signal voltage leaves its calibrated window. The most common physical causes are a contaminated MAF sensing element, unmetered air entering the intake downstream of the sensor, and chafed or corroded wiring in the sensor's 5V reference / signal-return circuit.
fix_title: Clean MAF sensing element and inspect intake ducting
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Remove the intake duct clamp and unplug the MAF connector • 2. Remove the sensor with a security Torx bit • 3. Spray the hot-wire element with dedicated MAF cleaner (never touch it) • 4. Inspect the duct for cracks or loose clamps downstream of the sensor • 5. Reinstall, clear the code, and road test

short_description: Mass or Volume Air Flow Sensor A Circuit Range/Performance
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0101 (Mass or Volume Air Flow Sensor A Circuit Range/Performance) sets when the ECM detects an implausible signal from the mass air flow sensor — reported airflow disagrees with throttle position and engine speed, or the signal voltage leaves its calibrated window. The most common physical causes are a contaminated MAF sensing element, unmetered air entering the intake downstream of the sensor, and chafed or corroded wiring in the sensor's 5V reference / signal-return circuit.
fix_title: Replace mass air flow sensor
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Disconnect the battery negative terminal • 2. Unplug the MAF harness connector and remove the sensor screws • 3. Install the replacement sensor without touching the element • 4. Reconnect and perform an ECU idle relearn if the platform requires it

short_description: Mass or Volume Air Flow Sensor A Circuit Range/Performance
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0101 (Mass or Volume Air Flow Sensor A Circuit Range/Performance) sets when the ECM detects an implausible signal from the mass air flow sensor — reported airflow disagrees with throttle position and engine speed, or the signal voltage leaves its calibrated window. The most common physical causes are a contaminated MAF sensing element, unmetered air entering the intake downstream of the sensor, and chafed or corroded wiring in the sensor's 5V reference / signal-return circuit.
fix_title: Repair intake air leak downstream of MAF
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Smoke-test or spray-test the intake tract between the MAF and throttle body • 2. Replace cracked intake boots and hardened couplers • 3. Torque all clamps and verify fuel trims return toward 0% at idle

short_description: Mass or Volume Air Flow Sensor A Circuit Range/Performance
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0101 (Mass or Volume Air Flow Sensor A Circuit Range/Performance) sets when the ECM detects an implausible signal from the mass air flow sensor — reported airflow disagrees with throttle position and engine speed, or the signal voltage leaves its calibrated window. The most common physical causes are a contaminated MAF sensing element, unmetered air entering the intake downstream of the sensor, and chafed or corroded wiring in the sensor's 5V reference / signal-return circuit.
fix_title: Diagnose and repair MAF circuit wiring
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Back-probe the 5V reference, signal, and ground at the MAF connector • 2. Perform a voltage-drop test on each leg back to the ECM • 3. Repair chafed conductors with solder and heat-shrink • 4. Confirm live airflow (g/s) tracks RPM on a scan tool

short_description: Manifold Absolute Pressure/Barometric Pressure Sensor Circuit Range/Performance
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0106 (Manifold Absolute Pressure/Barometric Pressure Sensor Circuit Range/Performance) sets when the ECM sees an open, shorted, or implausible voltage from the MAP/barometric pressure sensor circuit, or the reading is simply too low for the current altitude. Causes are a cracked or oil-fouled vacuum port, a failed sensor element, or damaged sensor wiring.
fix_title: Inspect and clean MAP sensor vacuum port
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Locate the MAP sensor on the intake manifold • 2. Remove and inspect the vacuum port for oil or debris • 3. Clean the port and sensor housing • 4. Reinstall and clear codes

short_description: Manifold Absolute Pressure/Barometric Pressure Sensor Circuit Range/Performance
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0106 (Manifold Absolute Pressure/Barometric Pressure Sensor Circuit Range/Performance) sets when the ECM sees an open, shorted, or implausible voltage from the MAP/barometric pressure sensor circuit, or the reading is simply too low for the current altitude. Causes are a cracked or oil-fouled vacuum port, a failed sensor element, or damaged sensor wiring.
fix_title: Replace MAP/barometric pressure sensor
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Unplug the sensor connector • 2. Remove the sensor from the manifold or its remote mount • 3. Install the replacement sensor with a new O-ring • 4. Clear codes and verify reading against known altitude/barometric baseline

short_description: Manifold Absolute Pressure/Barometric Pressure Sensor Circuit Range/Performance
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0106 (Manifold Absolute Pressure/Barometric Pressure Sensor Circuit Range/Performance) sets when the ECM sees an open, shorted, or implausible voltage from the MAP/barometric pressure sensor circuit, or the reading is simply too low for the current altitude. Causes are a cracked or oil-fouled vacuum port, a failed sensor element, or damaged sensor wiring.
fix_title: Repair MAP sensor circuit wiring
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Back-probe the 5V reference, signal, and ground at the connector • 2. Voltage-drop test each leg back to the ECM • 3. Repair chafed or corroded conductors with heat-shrink splices • 4. Confirm signal tracks vacuum/boost changes smoothly

short_description: Intake Air Temperature Sensor 1 Circuit High Bank 1
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0113 (Intake Air Temperature Sensor 1 Circuit High Bank 1) sets when the ECM sees an open, shorted, or implausible resistance from an intake air temperature sensor (any position/bank), or a correlation mismatch against a related ambient or fuel temperature signal. Causes are a corroded connector, a failed thermistor element, or chafed harness wiring near the intake tract.
fix_title: Inspect and clean IAT sensor connector
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Locate the IAT sensor in the intake tube or integrated into the MAF housing • 2. Unplug and inspect for corrosion or debris • 3. Clean contacts and apply dielectric grease • 4. Reconnect and clear codes

short_description: Intake Air Temperature Sensor 1 Circuit High Bank 1
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0113 (Intake Air Temperature Sensor 1 Circuit High Bank 1) sets when the ECM sees an open, shorted, or implausible resistance from an intake air temperature sensor (any position/bank), or a correlation mismatch against a related ambient or fuel temperature signal. Causes are a corroded connector, a failed thermistor element, or chafed harness wiring near the intake tract.
fix_title: Replace intake air temperature sensor
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Locate the affected IAT sensor per the bank/position in the code • 2. Disconnect and remove the sensor from its bore • 3. Install the replacement sensor with a new seal • 4. Clear codes and verify reading tracks ambient at cold start

short_description: Intake Air Temperature Sensor 1 Circuit High Bank 1
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0113 (Intake Air Temperature Sensor 1 Circuit High Bank 1) sets when the ECM sees an open, shorted, or implausible resistance from an intake air temperature sensor (any position/bank), or a correlation mismatch against a related ambient or fuel temperature signal. fix_title: Repair IAT sensor circuit wiring
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Measure sensor resistance at the connector against a temperature/resistance chart • 2. Voltage-drop test the circuit back to the ECM • 3. Repair chafed or corroded wiring near the intake tract with heat-shrink splices • 4. Confirm stable readings across temperature swings

short_description: Engine Coolant Temperature Sensor 1 Circuit High
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0118 (Engine Coolant Temperature Sensor 1 Circuit High) sets when the ECM sees an open, shorted, or implausible resistance from an engine or radiator coolant temperature sensor, or a correlation mismatch between redundant coolant/oil/ambient temperature signals. Causes are a corroded connector, a failed thermistor element, low coolant exposing the sensor tip, or chafed wiring.
fix_title: Top off coolant and inspect ECT sensor connector
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Check coolant level and top off with the correct spec coolant • 2. Unplug the ECT sensor connector and inspect for corrosion • 3. Clean contacts and reconnect • 4. Clear codes and monitor warm-up temperature curve

short_description: Engine Coolant Temperature Sensor 1 Circuit High
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0118 (Engine Coolant Temperature Sensor 1 Circuit High) sets when the ECM sees an open, shorted, or implausible resistance from an engine or radiator coolant temperature sensor, or a correlation mismatch between redundant coolant/oil/ambient temperature signals. Causes are a corroded connector, a failed thermistor element, low coolant exposing the sensor tip, or chafed wiring.
fix_title: Replace engine/radiator coolant temperature sensor
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Relieve cooling system pressure and drain coolant to below sensor level • 2. Remove the sensor from the head, housing, or radiator tank • 3. Install the replacement sensor with sealant or new seal as specified • 4. Refill coolant, bleed air, and clear codes

short_description: Engine Coolant Temperature Sensor 1 Circuit High
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0118 (Engine Coolant Temperature Sensor 1 Circuit High) sets when the ECM sees an open, shorted, or implausible resistance from an engine or radiator coolant temperature sensor, or a correlation mismatch between redundant coolant/oil/ambient temperature signals. Causes are a corroded connector, a failed thermistor element, low coolant exposing the sensor tip, or chafed wiring.
fix_title: Repair coolant temperature sensor wiring
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Measure sensor resistance at the connector against a temperature/resistance chart • 2. Voltage-drop test the circuit back to the ECM • 3. Repair chafed or corroded wiring with heat-shrink splices • 4. Confirm reading tracks actual coolant temperature

short_description: Throttle/Pedal Position Sensor/Switch A Circuit Range/Performance
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0121 (Throttle/Pedal Position Sensor/Switch A Circuit Range/Performance) sets when the ECM detects an open, shorted, or out-of-range voltage, or an implausible rate of change, from a throttle body or accelerator pedal position sensor channel. Causes are a worn sensor track, a corroded connector, or chafed wiring between the sensor and the ECM.
fix_title: Inspect and clean throttle/pedal position sensor connector
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Locate the throttle body or accelerator pedal assembly connector • 2. Unplug and inspect for corrosion or bent pins • 3. Clean contacts and reseat firmly • 4. Clear codes and verify smooth voltage sweep

short_description: Throttle/Pedal Position Sensor/Switch A Circuit Range/Performance
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0121 (Throttle/Pedal Position Sensor/Switch A Circuit Range/Performance) sets when the ECM detects an open, shorted, or out-of-range voltage, or an implausible rate of change, from a throttle body or accelerator pedal position sensor channel. Causes are a worn sensor track, a corroded connector, or chafed wiring between the sensor and the ECM.
fix_title: Replace throttle body or pedal position sensor assembly
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Unplug the connector and remove the mounting fasteners • 2. Install the replacement throttle body or pedal assembly • 3. Reconnect and perform a throttle relearn if required • 4. Clear codes and verify all channels track together

short_description: Throttle/Pedal Position Sensor/Switch A Circuit Range/Performance
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0121 (Throttle/Pedal Position Sensor/Switch A Circuit Range/Performance) sets when the ECM detects an open, shorted, or out-of-range voltage, or an implausible rate of change, from a throttle body or accelerator pedal position sensor channel. Causes are a worn sensor track, a corroded connector, or chafed wiring between the sensor and the ECM.
fix_title: Repair throttle/pedal position sensor wiring
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Back-probe the reference, signal, and ground circuits at the connector • 2. Voltage-drop test each leg back to the ECM • 3. Repair chafed conductors with heat-shrink splices • 4. Confirm all sensor channels agree throughout the pedal/throttle sweep

short_description: Coolant Thermostat (Coolant Temperature Below Thermostat Regulating Temperature)
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0128 (Coolant Thermostat (Coolant Temperature Below Thermostat Regulating Temperature)) sets when engine coolant fails to reach or maintain its regulating temperature within the expected time, or coolant flow/circulation is judged insufficient. Causes are a thermostat stuck open, a low coolant level, a failing water pump, or a chronically cold operating condition combined with a stuck-open thermostat.
fix_title: Check coolant level and inspect for external leaks
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Check coolant level cold and top off to spec • 2. Inspect hoses, radiator, and water pump weep hole for leaks • 3. Repair any obvious external leak source • 4. Clear codes and monitor warm-up time

short_description: Coolant Thermostat (Coolant Temperature Below Thermostat Regulating Temperature)
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0128 (Coolant Thermostat (Coolant Temperature Below Thermostat Regulating Temperature)) sets when engine coolant fails to reach or maintain its regulating temperature within the expected time, or coolant flow/circulation is judged insufficient. Causes are a thermostat stuck open, a low coolant level, a failing water pump, or a chronically cold operating condition combined with a stuck-open thermostat.
fix_title: Replace stuck-open thermostat
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Drain coolant below the thermostat housing • 2. Remove the housing and replace the thermostat and gasket • 3. Refill coolant and bleed air from the system • 4. Confirm engine reaches operating temperature within expected time

short_description: Coolant Thermostat (Coolant Temperature Below Thermostat Regulating Temperature)
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0128 (Coolant Thermostat (Coolant Temperature Below Thermostat Regulating Temperature)) sets when engine coolant fails to reach or maintain its regulating temperature within the expected time, or coolant flow/circulation is judged insufficient. Causes are a thermostat stuck open, a low coolant level, a failing water pump, or a chronically cold operating condition combined with a stuck-open thermostat.
fix_title: Replace failing water pump
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Drain coolant and remove drive components to access the water pump • 2. Remove the worn pump and inspect the impeller and bearing • 3. Install the replacement pump with a new gasket • 4. Refill, bleed, and verify stable circulation and temperature

short_description: O2 Sensor Circuit Low Voltage Bank 1 Sensor 1
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0131 (O2 Sensor Circuit Low Voltage Bank 1 Sensor 1) sets when the ECM sees an open circuit, a stuck/biased voltage, or no signal activity from an oxygen sensor's signal circuit. Causes are a contaminated or worn-out sensor element, an exhaust leak near the sensor bung skewing readings, or damaged sensor wiring.
fix_title: Inspect for exhaust leaks near the O2 sensor
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Inspect the exhaust manifold and pipe joints near the sensor bung for leaks • 2. Listen and smoke-test for exhaust leaks upstream of the sensor • 3. Replace gaskets or tighten fasteners as needed • 4. Clear codes and monitor sensor voltage trace

short_description: O2 Sensor Circuit Low Voltage Bank 1 Sensor 1
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0131 (O2 Sensor Circuit Low Voltage Bank 1 Sensor 1) sets when the ECM sees an open circuit, a stuck/biased voltage, or no signal activity from an oxygen sensor's signal circuit. Causes are a contaminated or worn-out sensor element, an exhaust leak near the sensor bung skewing readings, or damaged sensor wiring.
fix_title: Replace oxygen sensor
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Identify the affected bank/sensor position • 2. Remove the sensor with an O2 sensor socket • 3. Apply anti-seize to threads (avoid the tip) and install the new sensor • 4. Clear codes and confirm a normal switching voltage trace

short_description: O2 Sensor Circuit Low Voltage Bank 1 Sensor 1
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0131 (O2 Sensor Circuit Low Voltage Bank 1 Sensor 1) sets when the ECM sees an open circuit, a stuck/biased voltage, or no signal activity from an oxygen sensor's signal circuit. Causes are a contaminated or worn-out sensor element, an exhaust leak near the sensor bung skewing readings, or damaged sensor wiring.
fix_title: Repair O2 sensor signal circuit wiring
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Back-probe the signal and ground at the connector with the engine running • 2. Voltage-drop test the circuit back to the ECM • 3. Repair chafed or corroded wiring near the exhaust with high-temp splices • 4. Confirm proper switching activity

short_description: O2 Sensor Circuit Slow Response Bank 1 Sensor 1
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0133 (O2 Sensor Circuit Slow Response Bank 1 Sensor 1) sets when an oxygen sensor takes longer than the calibrated time to transition between rich and lean voltage states. This is typically a sensor element that has aged or been contaminated by silicone, oil ash, or coolant, though an exhaust leak that dilutes the sample can also slow the switching response.
fix_title: Inspect for contamination sources (oil, coolant, silicone) reaching the exhaust
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Check for oil consumption, coolant leaks, or recent use of silicone sealant near the intake/exhaust • 2. Repair the contamination source if found • 3. Inspect the sensor tip for a sooty or white/gray glassy coating • 4. Clear codes after addressing the source

short_description: O2 Sensor Circuit Slow Response Bank 1 Sensor 1
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0133 (O2 Sensor Circuit Slow Response Bank 1 Sensor 1) sets when an oxygen sensor takes longer than the calibrated time to transition between rich and lean voltage states. This is typically a sensor element that has aged or been contaminated by silicone, oil ash, or coolant, though an exhaust leak that dilutes the sample can also slow the switching response.
fix_title: Replace slow-responding oxygen sensor
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Identify the affected bank/sensor position • 2. Remove with an O2 sensor socket and inspect the old sensor tip for contamination • 3. Apply anti-seize and install the new sensor • 4. Clear codes and verify switching frequency improves

short_description: O2 Sensor Circuit Slow Response Bank 1 Sensor 1
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0133 (O2 Sensor Circuit Slow Response Bank 1 Sensor 1) sets when an oxygen sensor takes longer than the calibrated time to transition between rich and lean voltage states. This is typically a sensor element that has aged or been contaminated by silicone, oil ash, or coolant, though an exhaust leak that dilutes the sample can also slow the switching response.
fix_title: Repair exhaust leak diluting sensor sample
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Smoke-test the exhaust system upstream of the affected sensor • 2. Replace leaking gaskets or cracked pipe sections • 3. Torque fasteners to spec • 4. Clear codes and re-verify switching speed

short_description: O2 Sensor Heater Circuit Bank 1 Sensor 1
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0135 (O2 Sensor Heater Circuit Bank 1 Sensor 1) sets when the ECM detects an open, shorted, or out-of-spec resistance in the internal heater element of an oxygen sensor, or the heater fails to bring the sensor to operating temperature within the expected time. This is almost always a degraded heater element inside the O2 sensor itself, though a blown fuse, relay, or chafed heater wiring can also be responsible.
fix_title: Test O2 sensor heater circuit fuse and relay
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Locate the O2 heater fuse and relay in the underhood fuse box • 2. Inspect and test with a multimeter for continuity and proper switching • 3. Replace any blown fuse or failed relay • 4. Clear codes and verify heater current draw

short_description: O2 Sensor Heater Circuit Bank 1 Sensor 1
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0135 (O2 Sensor Heater Circuit Bank 1 Sensor 1) sets when the ECM detects an open, shorted, or out-of-spec resistance in the internal heater element of an oxygen sensor, or the heater fails to bring the sensor to operating temperature within the expected time. fix_title: Replace oxygen sensor with failed heater
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Identify the affected bank/sensor position from the code • 2. Unplug the connector and remove the sensor with an O2 sensor socket • 3. Apply anti-seize to threads (avoid the sensor tip) and install the new sensor • 4. Reconnect and clear codes

short_description: O2 Sensor Heater Circuit Bank 1 Sensor 1
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0135 (O2 Sensor Heater Circuit Bank 1 Sensor 1) sets when the ECM detects an open, shorted, or out-of-spec resistance in the internal heater element of an oxygen sensor, or the heater fails to bring the sensor to operating temperature within the expected time. short_description: System Too Lean Bank 1
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0171 (System Too Lean Bank 1) sets when the ECM must add more fuel than the calibrated limit allows to correct a lean condition on the indicated bank. The leading causes are a vacuum or intake air leak downstream of the MAF, a weak fuel pump or clogged filter, or a leaking exhaust manifold gasket confusing the upstream O2 sensor.
fix_title: Smoke-test intake for vacuum/air leaks
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Connect a smoke machine to the intake at idle • 2. Watch for smoke escaping at intake gaskets, PCV hoses, or brake booster line • 3. Replace any leaking gasket or hose • 4. Clear codes and verify fuel trims return toward 0%

short_description: System Too Lean Bank 1
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0171 (System Too Lean Bank 1) sets when the ECM must add more fuel than the calibrated limit allows to correct a lean condition on the indicated bank. The leading causes are a vacuum or intake air leak downstream of the MAF, a weak fuel pump or clogged filter, or a leaking exhaust manifold gasket confusing the upstream O2 sensor.
fix_title: Replace fuel filter and test fuel pump output
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Replace the fuel filter if serviceable • 2. Measure fuel pump pressure and volume against spec under load • 3. Replace a weak pump if it fails the test • 4. Clear codes and confirm rail pressure holds steady

short_description: System Too Lean Bank 1
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0171 (System Too Lean Bank 1) sets when the ECM must add more fuel than the calibrated limit allows to correct a lean condition on the indicated bank. The leading causes are a vacuum or intake air leak downstream of the MAF, a weak fuel pump or clogged filter, or a leaking exhaust manifold gasket confusing the upstream O2 sensor.
fix_title: Inspect and repair exhaust manifold gasket leak
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Inspect the exhaust manifold gasket and studs near the upstream O2 sensor for leaks • 2. Replace the gasket and any corroded studs • 3. Torque to spec in sequence • 4. Clear codes and re-verify trim correction

short_description: System Too Rich Bank 1
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0172 (System Too Rich Bank 1) sets when the ECM must remove more fuel than the calibrated limit allows to correct a rich condition on the indicated bank. Leading causes are a leaking fuel injector, a stuck-open fuel pressure regulator, a fouled MAF sensor overreporting airflow, or a failing fuel pressure sensor.
fix_title: Clean MAF sensor and inspect for false-high airflow readings
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Remove the MAF sensor and inspect the hot-wire element for contamination • 2. Clean with dedicated MAF cleaner (never touch the element) • 3. Reinstall and clear codes • 4. Compare live MAF g/s reading against expected values

short_description: System Too Rich Bank 1
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0172 (System Too Rich Bank 1) sets when the ECM must remove more fuel than the calibrated limit allows to correct a rich condition on the indicated bank. Leading causes are a leaking fuel injector, a stuck-open fuel pressure regulator, a fouled MAF sensor overreporting airflow, or a failing fuel pressure sensor.
fix_title: Test fuel injectors for leakage and replace as needed
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Perform a fuel injector leak-down/drip test • 2. Identify any injector leaking past its seat • 3. Replace the faulty injector(s) and seals • 4. Clear codes and verify trims return toward 0%

short_description: System Too Rich Bank 1
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0172 (System Too Rich Bank 1) sets when the ECM must remove more fuel than the calibrated limit allows to correct a rich condition on the indicated bank. Leading causes are a leaking fuel injector, a stuck-open fuel pressure regulator, a fouled MAF sensor overreporting airflow, or a failing fuel pressure sensor.
fix_title: Replace stuck-open fuel pressure regulator
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Depressurize the fuel system • 2. Remove the regulator and inspect the diaphragm/valve for sticking • 3. Install the replacement regulator with new seals • 4. Clear codes and confirm rail pressure holds at commanded target

short_description: Random/Multiple Cylinder Misfire Detected
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0300 (Random/Multiple Cylinder Misfire Detected) sets when the ECM's crankshaft-acceleration misfire monitor counts combustion events across multiple cylinders (or an undifferentiated pattern) beyond the calibrated threshold, including variants triggered by a low fuel level, a cold-start window, or a commanded fuel cutoff. Because no single cylinder is isolated, the cause is almost always something that affects the whole engine at once: a vacuum leak, worn spark plugs across the set, a weak fuel pump/low fuel level, or a base ignition timing/reluctor fault.
fix_title: Replace spark plugs and inspect ignition coils as a set
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Retrieve freeze-frame data to see which cylinders were flagged • 2. Remove and inspect all spark plugs for wear, fouling, or incorrect gap • 3. Replace the full set with OE-spec plugs • 4. Swap coils between cylinders if a weak coil is suspected • 5. Clear codes and monitor misfire counters on a drive cycle

short_description: Random/Multiple Cylinder Misfire Detected
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0300 (Random/Multiple Cylinder Misfire Detected) sets when the ECM's crankshaft-acceleration misfire monitor counts combustion events across multiple cylinders (or an undifferentiated pattern) beyond the calibrated threshold, including variants triggered by a low fuel level, a cold-start window, or a commanded fuel cutoff. Because no single cylinder is isolated, the cause is almost always something that affects the whole engine at once: a vacuum leak, worn spark plugs across the set, a weak fuel pump/low fuel level, or a base ignition timing/reluctor fault.
fix_title: Smoke-test intake and vacuum lines for a shared leak
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Connect a smoke machine at the intake tract with all accessory vacuum ports capped • 2. Watch for smoke escaping at intake gaskets, PCV hoses, or brake booster lines • 3. Replace any cracked hose or gasket found • 4. Clear the code and verify fuel trims return to normal

short_description: Random/Multiple Cylinder Misfire Detected
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0300 (Random/Multiple Cylinder Misfire Detected) sets when the ECM's crankshaft-acceleration misfire monitor counts combustion events across multiple cylinders (or an undifferentiated pattern) beyond the calibrated threshold, including variants triggered by a low fuel level, a cold-start window, or a commanded fuel cutoff. Because no single cylinder is isolated, the cause is almost always something that affects the whole engine at once: a vacuum leak, worn spark plugs across the set, a weak fuel pump/low fuel level, or a base ignition timing/reluctor fault.
fix_title: Verify fuel delivery and refuel if the tank is low
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Check the fuel gauge and refuel above one-quarter tank if low • 2. Scan for low fuel pressure or pump duty-cycle codes stored alongside the misfire • 3. Clear the code and complete a drive cycle to confirm it does not return

short_description: Random/Multiple Cylinder Misfire Detected
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0300 (Random/Multiple Cylinder Misfire Detected) sets when the ECM's crankshaft-acceleration misfire monitor counts combustion events across multiple cylinders (or an undifferentiated pattern) beyond the calibrated threshold, including variants triggered by a low fuel level, a cold-start window, or a commanded fuel cutoff. Because no single cylinder is isolated, the cause is almost always something that affects the whole engine at once: a vacuum leak, worn spark plugs across the set, a weak fuel pump/low fuel level, or a base ignition timing/reluctor fault.
fix_title: Test fuel pump output pressure and delivery volume
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Connect a fuel pressure gauge to the test port or rail • 2. Compare static and key-on pressure to spec • 3. Perform a timed volume test at the return line if accessible • 4. Replace a fuel pump that cannot hold pressure or deliver volume under load

short_description: Cylinder 1 Misfire Detected
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0301 (Cylinder 1 Misfire Detected) sets when the ECM's misfire monitor isolates repeated combustion events lost on one specific cylinder (or, for the unspecified variant, a single cylinder it cannot pin down). The diagnosis path is identical regardless of which cylinder number is reported: the fault lives in that cylinder's ignition, fuel delivery, or mechanical sealing, most commonly a fouled spark plug or failing coil, a clogged or leaking injector, or low compression from a burnt valve or worn rings.
fix_title: Swap spark plug and ignition coil to confirm the cylinder
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Remove the coil and plug from the misfiring cylinder • 2. Inspect the plug for fouling, wear, or an incorrect gap • 3. Swap the coil with a known-good adjacent cylinder's coil • 4. Clear the code and see if the misfire follows the coil or stays with the cylinder • 5. Replace the plug and/or coil based on the result

short_description: Cylinder 1 Misfire Detected
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0301 (Cylinder 1 Misfire Detected) sets when the ECM's misfire monitor isolates repeated combustion events lost on one specific cylinder (or, for the unspecified variant, a single cylinder it cannot pin down). The diagnosis path is identical regardless of which cylinder number is reported: the fault lives in that cylinder's ignition, fuel delivery, or mechanical sealing, most commonly a fouled spark plug or failing coil, a clogged or leaking injector, or low compression from a burnt valve or worn rings.
fix_title: Test and clean or replace the cylinder's fuel injector
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Perform an injector balance or ohms test on the affected cylinder • 2. Swap the injector with a neighboring cylinder to see if the misfire follows • 3. Clean the injector on an ultrasonic bench or replace it • 4. Reinstall with new O-rings and clear codes

short_description: Cylinder 1 Misfire Detected
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0301 (Cylinder 1 Misfire Detected) sets when the ECM's misfire monitor isolates repeated combustion events lost on one specific cylinder (or, for the unspecified variant, a single cylinder it cannot pin down). The diagnosis path is identical regardless of which cylinder number is reported: the fault lives in that cylinder's ignition, fuel delivery, or mechanical sealing, most commonly a fouled spark plug or failing coil, a clogged or leaking injector, or low compression from a burnt valve or worn rings.
fix_title: Run a cylinder compression or leak-down test
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Disable fuel and ignition for a dry compression test on all cylinders • 2. Compare the flagged cylinder's reading against its neighbors • 3. Add oil and retest (wet test) to isolate rings vs. valves • 4. Follow up with a leak-down test to confirm the exact leak path

short_description: Cylinder 1 Misfire Detected
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0301 (Cylinder 1 Misfire Detected) sets when the ECM's misfire monitor isolates repeated combustion events lost on one specific cylinder (or, for the unspecified variant, a single cylinder it cannot pin down). The diagnosis path is identical regardless of which cylinder number is reported: the fault lives in that cylinder's ignition, fuel delivery, or mechanical sealing, most commonly a fouled spark plug or failing coil, a clogged or leaking injector, or low compression from a burnt valve or worn rings.
fix_title: Inspect and replace a leaking or damaged cylinder head gasket/valve
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Confirm the leak-down result points to a head gasket or valve seat • 2. Remove the valve cover/head as required for access • 3. Replace the head gasket or recondition the valve seat • 4. Reassemble to torque spec and verify compression returns to spec

short_description: Cylinder 2 Misfire Detected
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0302 (Cylinder 2 Misfire Detected) sets when the ECM's misfire monitor isolates repeated combustion events lost on one specific cylinder (or, for the unspecified variant, a single cylinder it cannot pin down). The diagnosis path is identical regardless of which cylinder number is reported: the fault lives in that cylinder's ignition, fuel delivery, or mechanical sealing, most commonly a fouled spark plug or failing coil, a clogged or leaking injector, or low compression from a burnt valve or worn rings.
fix_title: Swap spark plug and ignition coil to confirm the cylinder
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Remove the coil and plug from the misfiring cylinder • 2. Inspect the plug for fouling, wear, or an incorrect gap • 3. Swap the coil with a known-good adjacent cylinder's coil • 4. Clear the code and see if the misfire follows the coil or stays with the cylinder • 5. Replace the plug and/or coil based on the result

short_description: Cylinder 2 Misfire Detected
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0302 (Cylinder 2 Misfire Detected) sets when the ECM's misfire monitor isolates repeated combustion events lost on one specific cylinder (or, for the unspecified variant, a single cylinder it cannot pin down). The diagnosis path is identical regardless of which cylinder number is reported: the fault lives in that cylinder's ignition, fuel delivery, or mechanical sealing, most commonly a fouled spark plug or failing coil, a clogged or leaking injector, or low compression from a burnt valve or worn rings.
fix_title: Test and clean or replace the cylinder's fuel injector
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Perform an injector balance or ohms test on the affected cylinder • 2. Swap the injector with a neighboring cylinder to see if the misfire follows • 3. Clean the injector on an ultrasonic bench or replace it • 4. Reinstall with new O-rings and clear codes

short_description: Knock/Combustion Vibration Sensor A Circuit
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0325 (Knock/Combustion Vibration Sensor A Circuit) sets when the ECM detects an open circuit, out-of-range voltage, or a total loss of signal from a knock (combustion vibration) sensor used to retard timing and protect the engine from detonation, or when the overall knock control system itself reports an internal error. Physical causes are a failed piezoelectric sensor element, an improperly torqued sensor (knock sensors are torque-sensitive and read incorrectly if over- or under-tightened), or chafed/corroded wiring between the sensor and the ECM.
fix_title: Inspect and reseat the knock sensor connector
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Locate the knock sensor(s), usually threaded into the block under the intake manifold • 2. Unplug and inspect for oil contamination or corroded pins • 3. Clean contacts and reseat the connector • 4. Clear the code and monitor knock counts on a scan tool

short_description: Knock/Combustion Vibration Sensor A Circuit
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0325 (Knock/Combustion Vibration Sensor A Circuit) sets when the ECM detects an open circuit, out-of-range voltage, or a total loss of signal from a knock (combustion vibration) sensor used to retard timing and protect the engine from detonation, or when the overall knock control system itself reports an internal error. Physical causes are a failed piezoelectric sensor element, an improperly torqued sensor (knock sensors are torque-sensitive and read incorrectly if over- or under-tightened), or chafed/corroded wiring between the sensor and the ECM.
fix_title: Replace the knock sensor at correct torque spec
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Remove the intake manifold or components blocking sensor access • 2. Unbolt the failed knock sensor • 3. Install the replacement and torque to the exact factory spec (over- or under-torquing causes false readings) • 4. Reconnect, clear codes, and verify signal activity while revving the engine

short_description: Knock/Combustion Vibration Sensor A Circuit
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0325 (Knock/Combustion Vibration Sensor A Circuit) sets when the ECM detects an open circuit, out-of-range voltage, or a total loss of signal from a knock (combustion vibration) sensor used to retard timing and protect the engine from detonation, or when the overall knock control system itself reports an internal error. Physical causes are a failed piezoelectric sensor element, an improperly torqued sensor (knock sensors are torque-sensitive and read incorrectly if over- or under-tightened), or chafed/corroded wiring between the sensor and the ECM.
fix_title: Repair knock sensor circuit wiring
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Back-probe the signal and shield/ground legs at the sensor connector • 2. Voltage-drop test the harness back to the ECM • 3. Repair chafed or corroded conductors with soldered, heat-shrunk, shielded splices • 4. Confirm a clean knock signal waveform on a scope

short_description: Knock/Combustion Vibration Sensor A Circuit
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0325 (Knock/Combustion Vibration Sensor A Circuit) sets when the ECM detects an open circuit, out-of-range voltage, or a total loss of signal from a knock (combustion vibration) sensor used to retard timing and protect the engine from detonation, or when the overall knock control system itself reports an internal error. Physical causes are a failed piezoelectric sensor element, an improperly torqued sensor (knock sensors are torque-sensitive and read incorrectly if over- or under-tightened), or chafed/corroded wiring between the sensor and the ECM.
fix_title: Diagnose knock control module/ECM internal fault
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Confirm all sensors and wiring test good in isolation • 2. Check for a shared power/ground fault affecting the knock control circuit • 3. Update or reflash ECM software if a known knock-strategy bulletin applies • 4. Replace the ECM only after all external causes are ruled out

short_description: Crankshaft Position Sensor A Circuit
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0335 (Crankshaft Position Sensor A Circuit) sets when the ECM sees an open circuit, out-of-range voltage, no signal, or an erratic pulse pattern from a crankshaft position sensor (or, on older distributor-based ignition systems, the distributor's engine-speed pickup that serves the same function). Common causes are a sensor with too large an air gap to the reluctor wheel, metal debris bridging the sensor tip, a corroded connector, or chafed wiring between the sensor and the ECM.
fix_title: Inspect and reseat the crankshaft position sensor connector
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Locate the sensor near the crank pulley or bellhousing • 2. Unplug and inspect for oil intrusion or corroded pins • 3. Clean contacts and reseat until it clicks • 4. Clear the code and crank the engine to verify a start

short_description: Crankshaft Position Sensor A Circuit
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0335 (Crankshaft Position Sensor A Circuit) sets when the ECM sees an open circuit, out-of-range voltage, no signal, or an erratic pulse pattern from a crankshaft position sensor (or, on older distributor-based ignition systems, the distributor's engine-speed pickup that serves the same function). Common causes are a sensor with too large an air gap to the reluctor wheel, metal debris bridging the sensor tip, a corroded connector, or chafed wiring between the sensor and the ECM.
fix_title: Replace the crankshaft position sensor
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Remove the sensor's retaining bolt and withdraw it from its bore • 2. Inspect the reluctor wheel/tone ring for damaged teeth or debris • 3. Install the replacement sensor, setting the air gap per spec if not self-adjusting • 4. Clear codes and confirm a stable RPM signal at idle and under load

short_description: Crankshaft Position Sensor A Circuit
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0335 (Crankshaft Position Sensor A Circuit) sets when the ECM sees an open circuit, out-of-range voltage, no signal, or an erratic pulse pattern from a crankshaft position sensor (or, on older distributor-based ignition systems, the distributor's engine-speed pickup that serves the same function). Common causes are a sensor with too large an air gap to the reluctor wheel, metal debris bridging the sensor tip, a corroded connector, or chafed wiring between the sensor and the ECM.
fix_title: Repair crankshaft position sensor circuit wiring
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Back-probe the signal, shield, and ground legs at the sensor connector • 2. Voltage-drop test each leg back to the ECM • 3. Repair chafed or corroded conductors with soldered, heat-shrunk splices • 4. Confirm a clean signal waveform on a scope while cranking

short_description: Crankshaft Position Sensor A Circuit
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0335 (Crankshaft Position Sensor A Circuit) sets when the ECM sees an open circuit, out-of-range voltage, no signal, or an erratic pulse pattern from a crankshaft position sensor (or, on older distributor-based ignition systems, the distributor's engine-speed pickup that serves the same function). Common causes are a sensor with too large an air gap to the reluctor wheel, metal debris bridging the sensor tip, a corroded connector, or chafed wiring between the sensor and the ECM.
fix_title: Clean or replace a damaged reluctor wheel/tone ring
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Remove the sensor and inspect the reluctor wheel through the access bore or with the crank pulley/cover removed • 2. Clean off accumulated debris or rust • 3. Replace a reluctor wheel with bent, missing, or chipped teeth • 4. Reinstall the sensor, reset the gap, and verify a clean signal

short_description: Camshaft Position Sensor A Circuit Bank 1 or Single Sensor
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0340 (Camshaft Position Sensor A Circuit Bank 1 or Single Sensor) sets when the ECM sees an open circuit, out-of-range voltage, or an erratic signal from the primary camshaft position sensor used for cylinder identification and cam-to-crank correlation — distinct from a VVT phaser's own actuator-position feedback sensor. Common causes are a corroded connector, a worn or contaminated reluctor/trigger wheel on the camshaft, an incorrect air gap, or chafed wiring between the sensor and the ECM.
fix_title: Inspect and reseat the camshaft position sensor connector
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Locate the sensor at the valve cover, timing cover, or distributor housing per platform • 2. Unplug and inspect for oil intrusion or corroded pins • 3. Clean contacts and reseat fully • 4. Clear the code and verify the engine restarts promptly

short_description: Camshaft Position Sensor A Circuit Bank 1 or Single Sensor
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0340 (Camshaft Position Sensor A Circuit Bank 1 or Single Sensor) sets when the ECM sees an open circuit, out-of-range voltage, or an erratic signal from the primary camshaft position sensor used for cylinder identification and cam-to-crank correlation — distinct from a VVT phaser's own actuator-position feedback sensor. Common causes are a corroded connector, a worn or contaminated reluctor/trigger wheel on the camshaft, an incorrect air gap, or chafed wiring between the sensor and the ECM.
fix_title: Replace the camshaft position sensor
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Remove the sensor's retaining bolt and withdraw it from its bore • 2. Inspect the O-ring and trigger wheel for wear or debris • 3. Install the replacement sensor with a fresh O-ring • 4. Clear codes and confirm cylinder identification on a scan tool

short_description: Camshaft Position Sensor A Circuit Bank 1 or Single Sensor
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0340 (Camshaft Position Sensor A Circuit Bank 1 or Single Sensor) sets when the ECM sees an open circuit, out-of-range voltage, or an erratic signal from the primary camshaft position sensor used for cylinder identification and cam-to-crank correlation — distinct from a VVT phaser's own actuator-position feedback sensor. Common causes are a corroded connector, a worn or contaminated reluctor/trigger wheel on the camshaft, an incorrect air gap, or chafed wiring between the sensor and the ECM.
fix_title: Repair camshaft position sensor circuit wiring
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Back-probe the signal, reference, and ground legs at the sensor connector • 2. Voltage-drop test each leg back to the ECM • 3. Repair chafed or corroded conductors with soldered, heat-shrunk splices • 4. Confirm a clean signal waveform on a scope while cranking

short_description: Camshaft Position Sensor A Circuit Bank 1 or Single Sensor
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0340 (Camshaft Position Sensor A Circuit Bank 1 or Single Sensor) sets when the ECM sees an open circuit, out-of-range voltage, or an erratic signal from the primary camshaft position sensor used for cylinder identification and cam-to-crank correlation — distinct from a VVT phaser's own actuator-position feedback sensor. Common causes are a corroded connector, a worn or contaminated reluctor/trigger wheel on the camshaft, an incorrect air gap, or chafed wiring between the sensor and the ECM.
fix_title: Inspect the camshaft trigger wheel for timing-related damage
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Remove valve cover components as needed to view the trigger wheel • 2. Check for chipped, bent, or slipped trigger wheel teeth from a prior timing chain/belt issue • 3. Correct the underlying timing fault and replace a damaged trigger wheel • 4. Reinstall the sensor and verify cam-crank correlation with a scan tool

short_description: Camshaft Position Sensor A Circuit Range/Performance Bank 1 or Single Sensor
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0341 (Camshaft Position Sensor A Circuit Range/Performance Bank 1 or Single Sensor) sets when the ECM sees an open circuit, out-of-range voltage, or an erratic signal from the primary camshaft position sensor used for cylinder identification and cam-to-crank correlation — distinct from a VVT phaser's own actuator-position feedback sensor. fix_title: Inspect and reseat the camshaft position sensor connector
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Locate the sensor at the valve cover, timing cover, or distributor housing per platform • 2. Unplug and inspect for oil intrusion or corroded pins • 3. Clean contacts and reseat fully • 4. Clear the code and verify the engine restarts promptly

short_description: Camshaft Position Sensor A Circuit Range/Performance Bank 1 or Single Sensor
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0341 (Camshaft Position Sensor A Circuit Range/Performance Bank 1 or Single Sensor) sets when the ECM sees an open circuit, out-of-range voltage, or an erratic signal from the primary camshaft position sensor used for cylinder identification and cam-to-crank correlation — distinct from a VVT phaser's own actuator-position feedback sensor. Common causes are a corroded connector, a worn or contaminated reluctor/trigger wheel on the camshaft, an incorrect air gap, or chafed wiring between the sensor and the ECM.
fix_title: Replace the camshaft position sensor
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Remove the sensor's retaining bolt and withdraw it from its bore • 2. Inspect the O-ring and trigger wheel for wear or debris • 3. Install the replacement sensor with a fresh O-ring • 4. Clear codes and confirm cylinder identification on a scan tool

short_description: Camshaft Position Sensor A Circuit Range/Performance Bank 1 or Single Sensor
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0341 (Camshaft Position Sensor A Circuit Range/Performance Bank 1 or Single Sensor) sets when the ECM sees an open circuit, out-of-range voltage, or an erratic signal from the primary camshaft position sensor used for cylinder identification and cam-to-crank correlation — distinct from a VVT phaser's own actuator-position feedback sensor. Common causes are a corroded connector, a worn or contaminated reluctor/trigger wheel on the camshaft, an incorrect air gap, or chafed wiring between the sensor and the ECM.
fix_title: Repair camshaft position sensor circuit wiring
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Back-probe the signal, reference, and ground legs at the sensor connector • 2. Voltage-drop test each leg back to the ECM • 3. Repair chafed or corroded conductors with soldered, heat-shrunk splices • 4. Confirm a clean signal waveform on a scope while cranking

short_description: Camshaft Position Sensor A Circuit Range/Performance Bank 1 or Single Sensor
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0341 (Camshaft Position Sensor A Circuit Range/Performance Bank 1 or Single Sensor) sets when the ECM sees an open circuit, out-of-range voltage, or an erratic signal from the primary camshaft position sensor used for cylinder identification and cam-to-crank correlation — distinct from a VVT phaser's own actuator-position feedback sensor. Common causes are a corroded connector, a worn or contaminated reluctor/trigger wheel on the camshaft, an incorrect air gap, or chafed wiring between the sensor and the ECM.
fix_title: Inspect the camshaft trigger wheel for timing-related damage
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Remove valve cover components as needed to view the trigger wheel • 2. Check for chipped, bent, or slipped trigger wheel teeth from a prior timing chain/belt issue • 3. Correct the underlying timing fault and replace a damaged trigger wheel • 4. Reinstall the sensor and verify cam-crank correlation with a scan tool

short_description: EGR A Flow Insufficient Detected
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0401 (EGR A Flow Insufficient Detected) sets when the ECM commands a specific EGR flow rate (valve A or valve B on dual-EGR platforms) and the measured intake or exhaust response indicates the actual flow is significantly less or more than commanded. Insufficient flow is most often a carbon-clogged EGR passage, valve, or cooler; excessive flow points to a valve stuck partially open or a failed position sensor causing the ECM to over-command it.
fix_title: Clean the EGR valve and passages of carbon buildup
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Remove the EGR valve from the intake manifold • 2. Scrape and solvent-clean carbon deposits from the valve pintle and seat • 3. Clean or snake out the connecting EGR passages/tube • 4. Reinstall with a new gasket and clear codes

short_description: EGR A Flow Insufficient Detected
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0401 (EGR A Flow Insufficient Detected) sets when the ECM commands a specific EGR flow rate (valve A or valve B on dual-EGR platforms) and the measured intake or exhaust response indicates the actual flow is significantly less or more than commanded. Insufficient flow is most often a carbon-clogged EGR passage, valve, or cooler; excessive flow points to a valve stuck partially open or a failed position sensor causing the ECM to over-command it.
fix_title: Inspect and clean the EGR cooler for restriction
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Locate and remove the EGR cooler from the exhaust/coolant circuit • 2. Inspect for soot buildup restricting the internal passages • 3. Clean with an approved solvent or replace if clogged/cracked internally • 4. Reinstall with new gaskets/clamps and clear codes

short_description: EGR A Flow Insufficient Detected
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0401 (EGR A Flow Insufficient Detected) sets when the ECM commands a specific EGR flow rate (valve A or valve B on dual-EGR platforms) and the measured intake or exhaust response indicates the actual flow is significantly less or more than commanded. fix_title: Test EGR valve position sensor tracking and valve travel
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Command the EGR valve through its full range with a scan tool • 2. Monitor commanded vs. actual position sensor feedback • 3. Replace the valve if it does not reach commanded positions or the sensor does not track smoothly • 4. Clear the code and verify flow response

short_description: EGR A Flow Insufficient Detected
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0401 (EGR A Flow Insufficient Detected) sets when the ECM commands a specific EGR flow rate (valve A or valve B on dual-EGR platforms) and the measured intake or exhaust response indicates the actual flow is significantly less or more than commanded. fix_title: Replace a mechanically stuck or worn EGR valve
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Confirm cleaning did not restore free valve movement • 2. Remove and replace the EGR valve assembly • 3. Install with a new gasket and torque to spec • 4. Clear codes and verify commanded flow is achieved
