short_description: EGR A Control Circuit/Open
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0403 (EGR A Control Circuit/Open) sets when the ECM detects an open circuit, out-of-range voltage, a learning-limit exceeded condition, or a mechanically stuck-open/stuck-closed report while driving an EGR control valve, an EGR throttle valve used to assist EGR flow, or the associated control processor. Causes are a failed EGR valve/throttle solenoid or motor, a corroded connector, carbon buildup mechanically jamming the valve, or damaged control wiring back to the ECM.
fix_title: Inspect and reseat the EGR valve/throttle connector
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Locate the EGR valve or EGR throttle connector • 2. Unplug and inspect for carbon contamination or corroded pins • 3. Clean contacts and reseat fully • 4. Clear the code and command the valve with a scan tool

short_description: EGR A Control Circuit/Open
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0403 (EGR A Control Circuit/Open) sets when the ECM detects an open circuit, out-of-range voltage, a learning-limit exceeded condition, or a mechanically stuck-open/stuck-closed report while driving an EGR control valve, an EGR throttle valve used to assist EGR flow, or the associated control processor. Causes are a failed EGR valve/throttle solenoid or motor, a corroded connector, carbon buildup mechanically jamming the valve, or damaged control wiring back to the ECM.
fix_title: Free a carbon-stuck EGR valve/throttle and clean the bore
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Remove the valve or throttle body from its mounting • 2. Scrape and solvent-clean carbon from the pintle/plate and bore • 3. Verify smooth full-range movement by hand before reinstalling • 4. Reinstall with a new gasket, clear codes, and command through full range

short_description: EGR A Control Circuit/Open
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0403 (EGR A Control Circuit/Open) sets when the ECM detects an open circuit, out-of-range voltage, a learning-limit exceeded condition, or a mechanically stuck-open/stuck-closed report while driving an EGR control valve, an EGR throttle valve used to assist EGR flow, or the associated control processor. Causes are a failed EGR valve/throttle solenoid or motor, a corroded connector, carbon buildup mechanically jamming the valve, or damaged control wiring back to the ECM.
fix_title: Replace the EGR control valve/throttle solenoid or motor
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Confirm cleaning did not restore proper electrical/mechanical response • 2. Remove and replace the valve/throttle assembly • 3. Install with a new gasket, torqued to spec • 4. Clear codes and verify commanded vs. actual position match

short_description: EGR A Control Circuit/Open
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0403 (EGR A Control Circuit/Open) sets when the ECM detects an open circuit, out-of-range voltage, a learning-limit exceeded condition, or a mechanically stuck-open/stuck-closed report while driving an EGR control valve, an EGR throttle valve used to assist EGR flow, or the associated control processor. Causes are a failed EGR valve/throttle solenoid or motor, a corroded connector, carbon buildup mechanically jamming the valve, or damaged control wiring back to the ECM.
fix_title: Repair EGR control circuit wiring back to the ECM
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Back-probe the control and feedback legs at the connector • 2. Voltage-drop test the harness back to the ECM • 3. Repair chafed or corroded conductors with soldered, heat-shrunk splices • 4. Clear codes and confirm clean commanded/actual position tracking

short_description: AIR System A Incorrect Flow Detected
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0411 (AIR System A Incorrect Flow Detected) sets when the ECM commands the secondary air injection system (used to inject fresh air into the exhaust during cold start to speed catalyst light-off and reduce emissions) and detects incorrect flow, an open/shorted switching valve circuit, or a control circuit fault. Causes are a seized or failed AIR pump, a stuck or corroded switching/check valve, a collapsed or leaking air delivery hose, or damaged wiring to the pump/valve.
fix_title: Inspect AIR delivery hoses and check valves
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Inspect the air delivery hose from the pump to the exhaust manifold for collapse, cracking, or disconnection • 2. Check the one-way check valve for exhaust blow-by damage • 3. Replace any damaged hose or check valve • 4. Clear the code and verify airflow with the system commanded on

short_description: AIR System A Incorrect Flow Detected
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0411 (AIR System A Incorrect Flow Detected) sets when the ECM commands the secondary air injection system (used to inject fresh air into the exhaust during cold start to speed catalyst light-off and reduce emissions) and detects incorrect flow, an open/shorted switching valve circuit, or a control circuit fault. Causes are a seized or failed AIR pump, a stuck or corroded switching/check valve, a collapsed or leaking air delivery hose, or damaged wiring to the pump/valve.
fix_title: Test and replace the AIR switching/combination valve
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Command the switching valve with a scan tool while listening/feeling for valve movement • 2. Inspect the valve and its connector for carbon or corrosion • 3. Replace a valve that does not switch cleanly • 4. Clear codes and verify flow direction changes correctly

short_description: AIR System A Incorrect Flow Detected
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0411 (AIR System A Incorrect Flow Detected) sets when the ECM commands the secondary air injection system (used to inject fresh air into the exhaust during cold start to speed catalyst light-off and reduce emissions) and detects incorrect flow, an open/shorted switching valve circuit, or a control circuit fault. Causes are a seized or failed AIR pump, a stuck or corroded switching/check valve, a collapsed or leaking air delivery hose, or damaged wiring to the pump/valve.
fix_title: Replace a seized or weak secondary air injection pump
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Command the AIR pump on with a scan tool and listen/feel for operation • 2. Check the pump relay and fuse if it does not run • 3. Replace a pump that is seized, noisy, or produces insufficient flow • 4. Clear codes and verify flow readings meet spec

short_description: AIR System A Incorrect Flow Detected
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0411 (AIR System A Incorrect Flow Detected) sets when the ECM commands the secondary air injection system (used to inject fresh air into the exhaust during cold start to speed catalyst light-off and reduce emissions) and detects incorrect flow, an open/shorted switching valve circuit, or a control circuit fault. Causes are a seized or failed AIR pump, a stuck or corroded switching/check valve, a collapsed or leaking air delivery hose, or damaged wiring to the pump/valve.
fix_title: Repair AIR system control circuit wiring
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Back-probe the control legs at the pump/valve connector • 2. Voltage-drop test the harness back to the ECM/relay • 3. Repair chafed or corroded conductors with soldered, heat-shrunk splices • 4. Clear codes and confirm the system responds to commands

short_description: Catalyst System Efficiency Below Threshold Bank 1
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0420 (Catalyst System Efficiency Below Threshold Bank 1) sets when the ECM compares pre- and post-catalyst oxygen sensor switching activity and finds the catalyst (or a specific catalyst brick on multi-brick systems) is no longer storing/releasing oxygen efficiently enough to meet emissions thresholds. The dominant cause is a catalyst that has aged out or been chemically poisoned (oil consumption, coolant leak into the exhaust, or leaded/high-sulfur fuel), though a persistent rich/lean fuel condition or an exhaust leak feeding false switching data can also trigger it.
fix_title: Inspect for exhaust leaks near the oxygen sensors
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Inspect the exhaust manifold, downpipe, and catalyst housing welds/gaskets for leaks near the O2 sensor bungs • 2. Repair any leak found with a new gasket or weld repair • 3. Clear the code and complete a catalyst monitor drive cycle

short_description: Catalyst System Efficiency Below Threshold Bank 1
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0420 (Catalyst System Efficiency Below Threshold Bank 1) sets when the ECM compares pre- and post-catalyst oxygen sensor switching activity and finds the catalyst (or a specific catalyst brick on multi-brick systems) is no longer storing/releasing oxygen efficiently enough to meet emissions thresholds. The dominant cause is a catalyst that has aged out or been chemically poisoned (oil consumption, coolant leak into the exhaust, or leaded/high-sulfur fuel), though a persistent rich/lean fuel condition or an exhaust leak feeding false switching data can also trigger it.
fix_title: Correct an underlying rich/lean fuel condition first
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Check for stored fuel trim, misfire, or O2 sensor codes alongside the catalyst code • 2. Repair the root fuel/ignition fault (it can chemically damage a good catalyst if left unaddressed) • 3. Clear the code and monitor fuel trims before assuming the catalyst itself is bad

short_description: Catalyst System Efficiency Below Threshold Bank 1
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0420 (Catalyst System Efficiency Below Threshold Bank 1) sets when the ECM compares pre- and post-catalyst oxygen sensor switching activity and finds the catalyst (or a specific catalyst brick on multi-brick systems) is no longer storing/releasing oxygen efficiently enough to meet emissions thresholds. The dominant cause is a catalyst that has aged out or been chemically poisoned (oil consumption, coolant leak into the exhaust, or leaded/high-sulfur fuel), though a persistent rich/lean fuel condition or an exhaust leak feeding false switching data can also trigger it.
fix_title: Replace the catalytic converter
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Confirm no upstream fault is masquerading as a catalyst fault • 2. Remove the old converter, cutting welded joints or unbolting flanges as applicable • 3. Install a CARB/EPA-compliant replacement converter for the application • 4. Clear codes and complete the catalyst monitor drive cycle to confirm the fix

short_description: Catalyst System Efficiency Below Threshold Bank 1
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0420 (Catalyst System Efficiency Below Threshold Bank 1) sets when the ECM compares pre- and post-catalyst oxygen sensor switching activity and finds the catalyst (or a specific catalyst brick on multi-brick systems) is no longer storing/releasing oxygen efficiently enough to meet emissions thresholds. The dominant cause is a catalyst that has aged out or been chemically poisoned (oil consumption, coolant leak into the exhaust, or leaded/high-sulfur fuel), though a persistent rich/lean fuel condition or an exhaust leak feeding false switching data can also trigger it.
fix_title: Address oil or coolant consumption poisoning the catalyst
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Check for blue (oil) or white sweet-smelling (coolant) exhaust smoke • 2. Diagnose and repair the oil-burning or coolant-leak source (valve seals, rings, head gasket) • 3. Replace the poisoned catalytic converter after the root cause is fixed • 4. Clear codes and verify the catalyst monitor passes

short_description: EVAP System
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0440 (EVAP System) sets when the EVAP leak-detection monitor cannot hold vacuum or pressure in the fuel vapor system within its small-leak threshold (typically a 0.020 in. equivalent orifice), including the tighter very-small-leak threshold and the fuel-tank-side/fresh-air-side variants used on natural-vacuum leak detection systems, or when the general EVAP system monitor itself fails. The dominant causes are a loose or worn fuel filler cap, a cracked EVAP hose, or a purge/vent solenoid that no longer seals.
fix_title: Inspect and reseat or replace fuel filler cap
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Inspect the filler cap gasket for cracks or dry rot • 2. Reseat the cap until it clicks • 3. Clear the code and complete an EVAP drive cycle • 4. Replace the cap if the code returns

short_description: EVAP System
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0440 (EVAP System) sets when the EVAP leak-detection monitor cannot hold vacuum or pressure in the fuel vapor system within its small-leak threshold (typically a 0.020 in. equivalent orifice), including the tighter very-small-leak threshold and the fuel-tank-side/fresh-air-side variants used on natural-vacuum leak detection systems, or when the general EVAP system monitor itself fails. The dominant causes are a loose or worn fuel filler cap, a cracked EVAP hose, or a purge/vent solenoid that no longer seals.
fix_title: Smoke-test EVAP system and replace leaking hose
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Connect a smoke machine to the EVAP service port • 2. Watch for smoke at hose unions, the canister, and the filler neck • 3. Replace the leaking hose or seal • 4. Re-test until the system holds pressure

short_description: EVAP System
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0440 (EVAP System) sets when the EVAP leak-detection monitor cannot hold vacuum or pressure in the fuel vapor system within its small-leak threshold (typically a 0.020 in. equivalent orifice), including the tighter very-small-leak threshold and the fuel-tank-side/fresh-air-side variants used on natural-vacuum leak detection systems, or when the general EVAP system monitor itself fails. The dominant causes are a loose or worn fuel filler cap, a cracked EVAP hose, or a purge/vent solenoid that no longer seals.
fix_title: Replace EVAP purge solenoid valve
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Locate the purge solenoid on the engine-side EVAP line • 2. Verify it holds vacuum when de-energized with a hand pump • 3. Replace the solenoid and clip the harness • 4. Clear the code and re-run the EVAP monitor

short_description: EVAP System
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0440 (EVAP System) sets when the EVAP leak-detection monitor cannot hold vacuum or pressure in the fuel vapor system within its small-leak threshold (typically a 0.020 in. equivalent orifice), including the tighter very-small-leak threshold and the fuel-tank-side/fresh-air-side variants used on natural-vacuum leak detection systems, or when the general EVAP system monitor itself fails. The dominant causes are a loose or worn fuel filler cap, a cracked EVAP hose, or a purge/vent solenoid that no longer seals.
fix_title: Replace charcoal canister / vent valve assembly
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Raise the vehicle and remove the canister shield • 2. Test the vent valve for sealing with a scan-tool actuator command • 3. Replace the canister or vent valve assembly • 4. Verify the leak-detection monitor passes

short_description: EVAP System Incorrect Purge Flow
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0441 (EVAP System Incorrect Purge Flow) sets when the ECM commands EVAP purge and the resulting flow through the purge line — measured by fuel trim shift, a purge flow sensor, or line pressure — is significantly higher or lower than expected for the commanded duty cycle, including the high-load purge line performance variant used on turbocharged/boosted platforms. Causes are a partially clogged purge line or canister restricting flow, a purge solenoid that leaks or does not open fully, or a collapsed/pinched purge hose under vacuum.
fix_title: Inspect the purge line for kinks, collapse, or restriction
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Trace the purge line from the canister to the intake/throttle body • 2. Look for a kinked, collapsed, or pinched section, especially near heat sources • 3. Replace the damaged hose section • 4. Clear the code and monitor purge flow on a scan tool

short_description: EVAP System Incorrect Purge Flow
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0441 (EVAP System Incorrect Purge Flow) sets when the ECM commands EVAP purge and the resulting flow through the purge line — measured by fuel trim shift, a purge flow sensor, or line pressure — is significantly higher or lower than expected for the commanded duty cycle, including the high-load purge line performance variant used on turbocharged/boosted platforms. Causes are a partially clogged purge line or canister restricting flow, a purge solenoid that leaks or does not open fully, or a collapsed/pinched purge hose under vacuum.
fix_title: Test and replace a leaking or sticking purge solenoid
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Command the purge solenoid with a scan tool while monitoring flow/vacuum • 2. Bench-test the solenoid for proper sealing when de-energized and full flow when energized • 3. Replace a solenoid that leaks or restricts flow • 4. Clear codes and verify purge flow matches command

short_description: EVAP System Incorrect Purge Flow
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0441 (EVAP System Incorrect Purge Flow) sets when the ECM commands EVAP purge and the resulting flow through the purge line — measured by fuel trim shift, a purge flow sensor, or line pressure — is significantly higher or lower than expected for the commanded duty cycle, including the high-load purge line performance variant used on turbocharged/boosted platforms. Causes are a partially clogged purge line or canister restricting flow, a purge solenoid that leaks or does not open fully, or a collapsed/pinched purge hose under vacuum.
fix_title: Clean or replace a restricted charcoal canister
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Remove the canister and inspect for saturated or degraded carbon media restricting flow • 2. Replace the canister if internally restricted • 3. Reinstall with new seals • 4. Clear the code and verify purge flow normalizes

short_description: EVAP System Incorrect Purge Flow
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0441 (EVAP System Incorrect Purge Flow) sets when the ECM commands EVAP purge and the resulting flow through the purge line — measured by fuel trim shift, a purge flow sensor, or line pressure — is significantly higher or lower than expected for the commanded duty cycle, including the high-load purge line performance variant used on turbocharged/boosted platforms. Causes are a partially clogged purge line or canister restricting flow, a purge solenoid that leaks or does not open fully, or a collapsed/pinched purge hose under vacuum.
fix_title: Replace the EVAP purge flow sensor if flow readings disagree with actual flow
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Compare the purge flow sensor's reading against a known commanded duty cycle • 2. Inspect the sensor's connector and mounting for damage • 3. Replace the sensor if it reads inconsistently with actual system behavior • 4. Clear codes and verify flow data now tracks correctly

short_description: EVAP System Leak Detected (small leak)
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0442 (EVAP System Leak Detected (small leak)) sets when the EVAP leak-detection monitor cannot hold vacuum or pressure in the fuel vapor system within its small-leak threshold (typically a 0.020 in. equivalent orifice), including the tighter very-small-leak threshold and the fuel-tank-side/fresh-air-side variants used on natural-vacuum leak detection systems, or when the general EVAP system monitor itself fails. The dominant causes are a loose or worn fuel filler cap, a cracked EVAP hose, or a purge/vent solenoid that no longer seals.
fix_title: Inspect and reseat or replace fuel filler cap
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Inspect the filler cap gasket for cracks or dry rot • 2. Reseat the cap until it clicks • 3. Clear the code and complete an EVAP drive cycle • 4. Replace the cap if the code returns

short_description: EVAP System Leak Detected (small leak)
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0442 (EVAP System Leak Detected (small leak)) sets when the EVAP leak-detection monitor cannot hold vacuum or pressure in the fuel vapor system within its small-leak threshold (typically a 0.020 in. equivalent orifice), including the tighter very-small-leak threshold and the fuel-tank-side/fresh-air-side variants used on natural-vacuum leak detection systems, or when the general EVAP system monitor itself fails. The dominant causes are a loose or worn fuel filler cap, a cracked EVAP hose, or a purge/vent solenoid that no longer seals.
fix_title: Smoke-test EVAP system and replace leaking hose
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Connect a smoke machine to the EVAP service port • 2. Watch for smoke at hose unions, the canister, and the filler neck • 3. Replace the leaking hose or seal • 4. Re-test until the system holds pressure

short_description: EVAP System Leak Detected (small leak)
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0442 (EVAP System Leak Detected (small leak)) sets when the EVAP leak-detection monitor cannot hold vacuum or pressure in the fuel vapor system within its small-leak threshold (typically a 0.020 in. equivalent orifice), including the tighter very-small-leak threshold and the fuel-tank-side/fresh-air-side variants used on natural-vacuum leak detection systems, or when the general EVAP system monitor itself fails. The dominant causes are a loose or worn fuel filler cap, a cracked EVAP hose, or a purge/vent solenoid that no longer seals.
fix_title: Replace EVAP purge solenoid valve
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Locate the purge solenoid on the engine-side EVAP line • 2. Verify it holds vacuum when de-energized with a hand pump • 3. Replace the solenoid and clip the harness • 4. Clear the code and re-run the EVAP monitor

short_description: EVAP System Leak Detected (small leak)
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0442 (EVAP System Leak Detected (small leak)) sets when the EVAP leak-detection monitor cannot hold vacuum or pressure in the fuel vapor system within its small-leak threshold (typically a 0.020 in. equivalent orifice), including the tighter very-small-leak threshold and the fuel-tank-side/fresh-air-side variants used on natural-vacuum leak detection systems, or when the general EVAP system monitor itself fails. The dominant causes are a loose or worn fuel filler cap, a cracked EVAP hose, or a purge/vent solenoid that no longer seals.
fix_title: Replace charcoal canister / vent valve assembly
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Raise the vehicle and remove the canister shield • 2. Test the vent valve for sealing with a scan-tool actuator command • 3. Replace the canister or vent valve assembly • 4. Verify the leak-detection monitor passes

short_description: EVAP System Vent Control Circuit
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0446 (EVAP System Vent Control Circuit) sets when the ECM detects an open, shorted, or out-of-range condition while driving the EVAP canister vent solenoid, the normally-open valve that must seal the system to atmosphere during a leak test. Causes are a failed solenoid coil, a corroded or unseated connector, debris/ice jamming the valve, or damaged wiring between the ECM and the solenoid.
fix_title: Inspect and reseat the vent solenoid connector
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Locate the vent solenoid, typically near the charcoal canister under the vehicle • 2. Unplug and inspect for corrosion, mud, or road-spray damage • 3. Clean contacts and reseat fully • 4. Clear the code and command the valve with a scan tool

short_description: EVAP System Vent Control Circuit
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0446 (EVAP System Vent Control Circuit) sets when the ECM detects an open, shorted, or out-of-range condition while driving the EVAP canister vent solenoid, the normally-open valve that must seal the system to atmosphere during a leak test. Causes are a failed solenoid coil, a corroded or unseated connector, debris/ice jamming the valve, or damaged wiring between the ECM and the solenoid.
fix_title: Replace the EVAP vent control solenoid
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Disconnect the vent solenoid's air inlet and electrical connector • 2. Verify sealing and switching function with a hand pump before condemning it • 3. Install the replacement solenoid • 4. Clear the code and re-run the EVAP monitor

short_description: EVAP System Vent Control Circuit
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0446 (EVAP System Vent Control Circuit) sets when the ECM detects an open, shorted, or out-of-range condition while driving the EVAP canister vent solenoid, the normally-open valve that must seal the system to atmosphere during a leak test. Causes are a failed solenoid coil, a corroded or unseated connector, debris/ice jamming the valve, or damaged wiring between the ECM and the solenoid.
fix_title: Clear debris or ice blocking the vent valve
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Locate the vent solenoid's fresh-air inlet filter/port • 2. Clear mud, leaves, or ice blocking the intake • 3. Verify free valve movement by hand or with a scan-tool command • 4. Clear the code and re-run the EVAP monitor

short_description: EVAP System Vent Control Circuit
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0446 (EVAP System Vent Control Circuit) sets when the ECM detects an open, shorted, or out-of-range condition while driving the EVAP canister vent solenoid, the normally-open valve that must seal the system to atmosphere during a leak test. Causes are a failed solenoid coil, a corroded or unseated connector, debris/ice jamming the valve, or damaged wiring between the ECM and the solenoid.
fix_title: Repair vent solenoid circuit wiring
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Back-probe the control leg at the vent solenoid connector • 2. Voltage-drop test the circuit back to the ECM and to chassis ground • 3. Repair chafed or corroded conductors with soldered, heat-shrunk splices • 4. Clear codes and confirm the vent valve opens and closes cleanly on command

short_description: EVAP System Leak Detected - Large Leak
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0455 (EVAP System Leak Detected - Large Leak) sets when the EVAP leak-detection monitor detects a leak far larger than the small-leak threshold (roughly equivalent to a 0.040 in. orifice or bigger), including the fuel-tank-side and fresh-air-side variants on natural-vacuum leak detection systems. This points to a substantially open path to atmosphere: a fuel cap left off or badly damaged, a disconnected or split EVAP hose, or a canister/valve that has failed open.
fix_title: Check for a missing, damaged, or off-track fuel filler cap
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Confirm the fuel cap is present, undamaged, and threaded on correctly • 2. Replace a cracked or missing cap • 3. Clear the code and complete an EVAP drive cycle

short_description: EVAP System Leak Detected - Large Leak
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0455 (EVAP System Leak Detected - Large Leak) sets when the EVAP leak-detection monitor detects a leak far larger than the small-leak threshold (roughly equivalent to a 0.040 in. orifice or bigger), including the fuel-tank-side and fresh-air-side variants on natural-vacuum leak detection systems. This points to a substantially open path to atmosphere: a fuel cap left off or badly damaged, a disconnected or split EVAP hose, or a canister/valve that has failed open.
fix_title: Locate and repair a disconnected or split EVAP hose
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Visually trace the EVAP hose routing from the tank to the canister and engine • 2. Look for a hose knocked loose, split, or chewed by rodents • 3. Reconnect or replace the damaged section • 4. Smoke-test to confirm the leak is closed

short_description: EVAP System Leak Detected - Large Leak
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0455 (EVAP System Leak Detected - Large Leak) sets when the EVAP leak-detection monitor detects a leak far larger than the small-leak threshold (roughly equivalent to a 0.040 in. orifice or bigger), including the fuel-tank-side and fresh-air-side variants on natural-vacuum leak detection systems. This points to a substantially open path to atmosphere: a fuel cap left off or badly damaged, a disconnected or split EVAP hose, or a canister/valve that has failed open.
fix_title: Replace a failed-open EVAP canister vent valve
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Command the vent valve closed with a scan tool and verify it holds vacuum with a hand pump • 2. Replace a valve that will not seal • 3. Reinstall and clear codes • 4. Re-run the EVAP monitor to confirm the large-leak fault clears

short_description: EVAP System Leak Detected - Large Leak
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0455 (EVAP System Leak Detected - Large Leak) sets when the EVAP leak-detection monitor detects a leak far larger than the small-leak threshold (roughly equivalent to a 0.040 in. orifice or bigger), including the fuel-tank-side and fresh-air-side variants on natural-vacuum leak detection systems. This points to a substantially open path to atmosphere: a fuel cap left off or badly damaged, a disconnected or split EVAP hose, or a canister/valve that has failed open.
fix_title: Pressure/vacuum-test the fuel tank and sending unit seal
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Apply controlled test pressure/vacuum to the fuel tank via the service port • 2. Monitor for a leak at the fuel sending unit seal or tank seam • 3. Replace the sending unit seal or repair the tank as needed • 4. Clear codes and confirm the system holds

short_description: Vehicle Speed Sensor A Circuit
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0500 (Vehicle Speed Sensor A Circuit) sets when the ECM loses, or receives an implausible, vehicle speed signal from the primary VSS/output-shaft speed source used for speedometer, cruise control, ABS coordination, and shift scheduling. The signal is usually a toothed-wheel/magnetic or Hall-effect pickup, so the common causes are a damaged tone ring, an air-gap or debris problem at the sensor tip, a corroded connector, or chafed sensor wiring shorted to power/ground.
fix_title: Clean tone ring and inspect sensor air gap
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Locate the VSS at the transmission output shaft or transfer case • 2. Remove the sensor and inspect the tip for metal debris • 3. Clean the toothed reluctor wheel with brake cleaner • 4. Reinstall with correct air-gap spacer if equipped and clear the code

short_description: Vehicle Speed Sensor A Circuit
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0500 (Vehicle Speed Sensor A Circuit) sets when the ECM loses, or receives an implausible, vehicle speed signal from the primary VSS/output-shaft speed source used for speedometer, cruise control, ABS coordination, and shift scheduling. The signal is usually a toothed-wheel/magnetic or Hall-effect pickup, so the common causes are a damaged tone ring, an air-gap or debris problem at the sensor tip, a corroded connector, or chafed sensor wiring shorted to power/ground.
fix_title: Repair or replace VSS connector and pigtail
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Unplug the VSS connector and check for corrosion or bent pins • 2. Clean contacts with electrical contact cleaner • 3. Replace a damaged pigtail with a soldered, heat-shrunk splice • 4. Verify a clean speed trace on a scan tool at a slow roll

short_description: Vehicle Speed Sensor A Circuit
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0500 (Vehicle Speed Sensor A Circuit) sets when the ECM loses, or receives an implausible, vehicle speed signal from the primary VSS/output-shaft speed source used for speedometer, cruise control, ABS coordination, and shift scheduling. The signal is usually a toothed-wheel/magnetic or Hall-effect pickup, so the common causes are a damaged tone ring, an air-gap or debris problem at the sensor tip, a corroded connector, or chafed sensor wiring shorted to power/ground.
fix_title: Replace vehicle speed sensor
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Disconnect the battery negative terminal • 2. Remove the sensor retaining bolt and pull the sensor from its bore • 3. Install the new sensor with a fresh O-ring seated fully • 4. Clear the code and confirm speedometer/cruise operation on a test drive

short_description: Vehicle Speed Sensor A Circuit
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0500 (Vehicle Speed Sensor A Circuit) sets when the ECM loses, or receives an implausible, vehicle speed signal from the primary VSS/output-shaft speed source used for speedometer, cruise control, ABS coordination, and shift scheduling. The signal is usually a toothed-wheel/magnetic or Hall-effect pickup, so the common causes are a damaged tone ring, an air-gap or debris problem at the sensor tip, a corroded connector, or chafed sensor wiring shorted to power/ground.
fix_title: Diagnose and repair VSS harness wiring
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Back-probe signal, reference, and ground at the ECM connector • 2. Trace the harness for chafe points near the transmission or subframe • 3. Repair with proper gauge wire and abrasion-resistant loom • 4. Road test through the full speed range while monitoring signal quality

short_description: Idle Control System
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0505 (Idle Control System) sets when commanded idle RPM (or, on diesel platforms, idle fuel quantity) does not match the ECM's target, or the idle air control actuator's circuit reports an open/out-of-range condition. Root causes are a carbon-fouled IAC valve or throttle bore restricting airflow, an unmetered vacuum/intake air leak that pushes idle high, a sticking throttle plate, or a failed IAC motor/solenoid and its wiring.
fix_title: Clean throttle body and IAC passage
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Remove the intake duct and IAC valve or throttle body cover • 2. Spray throttle-body cleaner into the idle air passage and throttle bore • 3. Wipe away carbon with a shop towel, avoiding the throttle plate seal edge • 4. Reinstall, reset idle relearn, and confirm idle stabilizes

short_description: Idle Control System
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0505 (Idle Control System) sets when commanded idle RPM (or, on diesel platforms, idle fuel quantity) does not match the ECM's target, or the idle air control actuator's circuit reports an open/out-of-range condition. Root causes are a carbon-fouled IAC valve or throttle bore restricting airflow, an unmetered vacuum/intake air leak that pushes idle high, a sticking throttle plate, or a failed IAC motor/solenoid and its wiring.
fix_title: Inspect and repair vacuum/intake leaks
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Inspect all vacuum hoses, PCV lines, and intake gaskets for cracks • 2. Smoke-test or use a propane/carb-cleaner leak check at idle • 3. Replace any hose, gasket, or clamp found leaking • 4. Verify idle RPM and fuel trims return to normal

short_description: Idle Control System
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0505 (Idle Control System) sets when commanded idle RPM (or, on diesel platforms, idle fuel quantity) does not match the ECM's target, or the idle air control actuator's circuit reports an open/out-of-range condition. Root causes are a carbon-fouled IAC valve or throttle bore restricting airflow, an unmetered vacuum/intake air leak that pushes idle high, a sticking throttle plate, or a failed IAC motor/solenoid and its wiring.
fix_title: Replace idle air control valve/motor
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Disconnect the battery negative terminal • 2. Unplug the IAC connector and remove its mounting screws • 3. Install the replacement valve with a new gasket • 4. Perform the ECM idle relearn procedure and road test

short_description: Idle Control System
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0505 (Idle Control System) sets when commanded idle RPM (or, on diesel platforms, idle fuel quantity) does not match the ECM's target, or the idle air control actuator's circuit reports an open/out-of-range condition. fix_title: Diagnose IAC circuit wiring at the ECM
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Back-probe the IAC driver circuits at the connector with a scan tool commanding sweep • 2. Voltage-drop test each leg back to the ECM • 3. Repair chafed or corroded conductors • 4. Confirm commanded vs actual idle counts track together

short_description: System Voltage Low
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0562 (System Voltage Low) sets when the ECM's own B+ sense circuit measures system voltage outside its normal window or fluctuating erratically, pointing at the vehicle's overall charging/electrical system rather than a single sensor. Causes are a weak or failing battery, a charging system undercharging or overcharging, corroded main power/ground straps, or a parasitic draw combined with marginal alternator output.
fix_title: Test battery condition and terminal connections
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Clean and tighten battery terminals and main ground straps • 2. Load-test the battery for cranking capacity • 3. Replace a battery that fails the load test • 4. Clear the code and recheck system voltage

short_description: System Voltage Low
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0562 (System Voltage Low) sets when the ECM's own B+ sense circuit measures system voltage outside its normal window or fluctuating erratically, pointing at the vehicle's overall charging/electrical system rather than a single sensor. Causes are a weak or failing battery, a charging system undercharging or overcharging, corroded main power/ground straps, or a parasitic draw combined with marginal alternator output.
fix_title: Test charging system output under load
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Measure voltage at idle, at 2000 RPM, and with accessories loaded (headlights, blower, defrost) • 2. Compare to the expected 13.5-14.7V charging range • 3. Note whether voltage is chronically low, high, or unstable • 4. Proceed to the alternator or wiring repair matching the finding

short_description: System Voltage Low
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0562 (System Voltage Low) sets when the ECM's own B+ sense circuit measures system voltage outside its normal window or fluctuating erratically, pointing at the vehicle's overall charging/electrical system rather than a single sensor. Causes are a weak or failing battery, a charging system undercharging or overcharging, corroded main power/ground straps, or a parasitic draw combined with marginal alternator output.
fix_title: Repair main power/ground straps and connections
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Inspect engine-to-body and battery-to-chassis ground straps for corrosion • 2. Clean or replace corroded straps and terminals • 3. Verify voltage drop across each strap is under 0.2V while cranking • 4. Clear the code and retest

short_description: System Voltage Low
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0562 (System Voltage Low) sets when the ECM's own B+ sense circuit measures system voltage outside its normal window or fluctuating erratically, pointing at the vehicle's overall charging/electrical system rather than a single sensor. fix_title: Diagnose parasitic draw or replace alternator
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Measure key-off parasitic current draw and pull fuses to isolate an excessive draw • 2. Bench-test or load-test the alternator output and diode ripple • 3. Replace the alternator if output is deficient or a diode has failed • 4. Recheck system voltage stability across a full drive cycle

short_description: Internal Control Module Memory Checksum Error
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0601 (Internal Control Module Memory Checksum Error) sets when a control module's (ECM, PCM, or TCM) internal self-test finds its memory (RAM/ROM/EEPROM/NVRAM/checksum), processor, or an internal signal-processing channel is not functioning correctly. Unlike a sensor circuit fault, this points at the module's own hardware, so the fix path is reflashing the module's software first and, if the fault returns, replacing the module rather than chasing external wiring.
fix_title: Verify battery/charging voltage before further diagnosis
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Confirm battery voltage is above 12.4V and charging system output is normal • 2. Low or unstable voltage during a reflash can itself corrupt module memory, mimicking this fault • 3. Correct any battery/charging issue found • 4. Clear the code and retest

short_description: Internal Control Module Memory Checksum Error
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0601 (Internal Control Module Memory Checksum Error) sets when a control module's (ECM, PCM, or TCM) internal self-test finds its memory (RAM/ROM/EEPROM/NVRAM/checksum), processor, or an internal signal-processing channel is not functioning correctly. Unlike a sensor circuit fault, this points at the module's own hardware, so the fix path is reflashing the module's software first and, if the fault returns, replacing the module rather than chasing external wiring.
fix_title: Reflash the control module to the latest calibration
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Connect a scan tool with manufacturer reprogramming access on a stable power supply • 2. Reflash the module with the latest available calibration • 3. Clear codes and cycle the ignition • 4. Drive-cycle and confirm the fault does not return

short_description: Internal Control Module Memory Checksum Error
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0601 (Internal Control Module Memory Checksum Error) sets when a control module's (ECM, PCM, or TCM) internal self-test finds its memory (RAM/ROM/EEPROM/NVRAM/checksum), processor, or an internal signal-processing channel is not functioning correctly. Unlike a sensor circuit fault, this points at the module's own hardware, so the fix path is reflashing the module's software first and, if the fault returns, replacing the module rather than chasing external wiring.
fix_title: Replace the control module
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Confirm the fault persists after a successful reflash, indicating a hardware failure • 2. Disconnect the battery and remove the affected module • 3. Install and program the replacement module with the correct VIN/calibration • 4. Clear codes and verify normal operation across all monitored channels

short_description: Transmission Control System (MIL Request)
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0700 (Transmission Control System (MIL Request)) sets when the TCM's overall self-check strategy — as opposed to any single sensor — requests the MIL, finds an electrical fault on its main harness, or judges its adaptive shift strategy is out of range. Causes are low or contaminated transmission fluid, a TCM software issue, an aftermarket electrical accessory disrupting the TCM's power/ground, or a wiring fault on the main TCM harness connector.
fix_title: Check transmission fluid level and condition
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Check fluid level and color per the platform's check procedure (dipstick or level plug) • 2. Top off or service fluid if low, dark, or burnt-smelling • 3. Clear the code and drive-cycle • 4. Recheck for the code returning

short_description: Transmission Control System (MIL Request)
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0700 (Transmission Control System (MIL Request)) sets when the TCM's overall self-check strategy — as opposed to any single sensor — requests the MIL, finds an electrical fault on its main harness, or judges its adaptive shift strategy is out of range. Causes are low or contaminated transmission fluid, a TCM software issue, an aftermarket electrical accessory disrupting the TCM's power/ground, or a wiring fault on the main TCM harness connector.
fix_title: Inspect main TCM harness connector
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Locate the main TCM connector (often at the transmission case or under the hood) • 2. Disconnect and inspect for corrosion, bent pins, or fluid intrusion • 3. Clean and reseat, or repair a damaged pin • 4. Clear the code and road test

short_description: Transmission Control System (MIL Request)
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0700 (Transmission Control System (MIL Request)) sets when the TCM's overall self-check strategy — as opposed to any single sensor — requests the MIL, finds an electrical fault on its main harness, or judges its adaptive shift strategy is out of range. Causes are low or contaminated transmission fluid, a TCM software issue, an aftermarket electrical accessory disrupting the TCM's power/ground, or a wiring fault on the main TCM harness connector.
fix_title: Reflash the TCM to the latest calibration
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Check for a manufacturer TSB covering this shift-quality/MIL-request code • 2. Reflash the TCM with the latest calibration if applicable • 3. Clear codes and perform any required adaptive relearn • 4. Road test through a full shift cycle

short_description: Transmission Range Sensor A Circuit (PRNDL Input)
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0705 (Transmission Range Sensor A Circuit (PRNDL Input)) sets when the TCM sees an open, shorted, or implausible signal from the transmission range sensor that reports gear-selector position (Park/Reverse/Neutral/Drive/Low) to the ECM/TCM. Causes are a maladjusted or worn range sensor at the shift shaft, transmission fluid contamination fouling the sensor, or chafed wiring at the connector on the transmission case.
fix_title: Adjust transmission range sensor
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Locate the range sensor on the shift shaft at the transmission case • 2. Loosen the sensor and align it to the neutral/park index mark per spec • 3. Re-torque and verify each gear selection is confirmed by the sensor • 4. Clear the code and road test

short_description: Transmission Range Sensor A Circuit (PRNDL Input)
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0705 (Transmission Range Sensor A Circuit (PRNDL Input)) sets when the TCM sees an open, shorted, or implausible signal from the transmission range sensor that reports gear-selector position (Park/Reverse/Neutral/Drive/Low) to the ECM/TCM. Causes are a maladjusted or worn range sensor at the shift shaft, transmission fluid contamination fouling the sensor, or chafed wiring at the connector on the transmission case.
fix_title: Repair range sensor circuit wiring
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Back-probe the range sensor circuits at the connector through each gear position • 2. Trace the harness for fluid intrusion or chafing at the case connector • 3. Repair with solder and heat-shrink, resealing the connector • 4. Confirm correct PRNDL reporting in every position

short_description: Transmission Range Sensor A Circuit (PRNDL Input)
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0705 (Transmission Range Sensor A Circuit (PRNDL Input)) sets when the TCM sees an open, shorted, or implausible signal from the transmission range sensor that reports gear-selector position (Park/Reverse/Neutral/Drive/Low) to the ECM/TCM. Causes are a maladjusted or worn range sensor at the shift shaft, transmission fluid contamination fouling the sensor, or chafed wiring at the connector on the transmission case.
fix_title: Replace transmission range sensor
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Disconnect the battery negative terminal • 2. Remove the shift linkage and unbolt the range sensor • 3. Install and align the new sensor per spec, then reconnect linkage • 4. Clear the code and verify PRNDL matches actual gear

short_description: Transmission Range Sensor A Circuit Range/Performance

short_description: Input/Turbine Shaft Speed Sensor A Circuit
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0715 (Input/Turbine Shaft Speed Sensor A Circuit) sets when the TCM loses, or receives an implausible, input/turbine shaft speed signal used for shift scheduling and torque converter clutch control. Causes are a damaged reluctor wheel, debris or metal shavings at the sensor tip, a corroded connector, or chafed wiring inside the transmission harness.
fix_title: Inspect sensor connector for fluid intrusion
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Locate the sensor connector at the transmission case • 2. Disconnect and inspect for fluid/corrosion in the pins • 3. Clean and reseat with a fresh seal • 4. Clear the code and road test

short_description: Input/Turbine Shaft Speed Sensor A Circuit
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0715 (Input/Turbine Shaft Speed Sensor A Circuit) sets when the TCM loses, or receives an implausible, input/turbine shaft speed signal used for shift scheduling and torque converter clutch control. Causes are a damaged reluctor wheel, debris or metal shavings at the sensor tip, a corroded connector, or chafed wiring inside the transmission harness.
fix_title: Replace input/turbine shaft speed sensor
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Disconnect the battery negative terminal • 2. Remove the sensor from the transmission case, noting fluid loss • 3. Install the new sensor with a fresh O-ring • 4. Top off fluid, clear the code, and verify signal on a scan tool

short_description: Input/Turbine Shaft Speed Sensor A Circuit
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0715 (Input/Turbine Shaft Speed Sensor A Circuit) sets when the TCM loses, or receives an implausible, input/turbine shaft speed signal used for shift scheduling and torque converter clutch control. Causes are a damaged reluctor wheel, debris or metal shavings at the sensor tip, a corroded connector, or chafed wiring inside the transmission harness.
fix_title: Repair internal/external transmission wiring harness
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Back-probe the sensor circuit at the TCM connector • 2. Trace the internal transmission harness for chafing (may require pan removal) • 3. Repair or replace the internal harness section • 4. Refill fluid, clear the code, and verify a clean speed trace

short_description: Output Shaft Speed Sensor Circuit
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0720 (Output Shaft Speed Sensor Circuit) sets when the TCM loses, receives an implausible, or loses direction information from the transmission output shaft speed sensor used for shift scheduling and vehicle speed cross-check. Causes are a damaged reluctor wheel, debris at the sensor tip, a corroded connector, or chafed internal transmission wiring.
fix_title: Inspect output shaft speed sensor connector
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Locate the sensor connector at the transmission tail housing/case • 2. Disconnect and inspect for fluid intrusion/corrosion • 3. Clean and reseat with a fresh seal • 4. Clear the code and road test

short_description: Output Shaft Speed Sensor Circuit
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0720 (Output Shaft Speed Sensor Circuit) sets when the TCM loses, receives an implausible, or loses direction information from the transmission output shaft speed sensor used for shift scheduling and vehicle speed cross-check. Causes are a damaged reluctor wheel, debris at the sensor tip, a corroded connector, or chafed internal transmission wiring.
fix_title: Replace output shaft speed sensor
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Raise the vehicle and locate the sensor at the transmission tail housing • 2. Remove its retaining bolt and withdraw the sensor, catching any fluid drip • 3. Install the replacement sensor with a new O-ring, seated fully • 4. Clear the code and confirm output speed tracks vehicle speed

short_description: Output Shaft Speed Sensor Circuit
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0720 (Output Shaft Speed Sensor Circuit) sets when the TCM loses, receives an implausible, or loses direction information from the transmission output shaft speed sensor used for shift scheduling and vehicle speed cross-check. Causes are a damaged reluctor wheel, debris at the sensor tip, a corroded connector, or chafed internal transmission wiring.
fix_title: Repair output shaft sensor wiring
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Back-probe the sensor circuit at the TCM connector • 2. Trace the internal/external harness for chafing • 3. Repair or replace the affected harness section • 4. Clear the code and verify a clean, correctly-directioned speed trace

short_description: Torque Converter Clutch Circuit Performance/Stuck Off
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0741 (Torque Converter Clutch Circuit Performance/Stuck Off) sets when the TCM detects an open circuit, out-of-range current, or a stuck-on/stuck-off performance fault while driving the torque converter clutch solenoid that locks the converter for fuel economy at cruise. Causes are a failed TCC solenoid, contaminated fluid causing the solenoid or clutch plate to stick, or damaged control wiring.
fix_title: Verify transmission fluid level before TCC diagnosis
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Check fluid level and condition • 2. Change fluid and filter if contaminated, since debris commonly sticks the TCC solenoid • 3. Clear the code and drive-cycle at steady highway speed • 4. Monitor for the code returning

short_description: Torque Converter Clutch Circuit Performance/Stuck Off
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0741 (Torque Converter Clutch Circuit Performance/Stuck Off) sets when the TCM detects an open circuit, out-of-range current, or a stuck-on/stuck-off performance fault while driving the torque converter clutch solenoid that locks the converter for fuel economy at cruise. Causes are a failed TCC solenoid, contaminated fluid causing the solenoid or clutch plate to stick, or damaged control wiring.
fix_title: Command the TCC solenoid with a scan-tool actuator test
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Connect a scan tool with bi-directional solenoid control • 2. Command the torque converter clutch solenoid on and off at a safe steady speed • 3. Confirm RPM/converter-slip PID responds to the command • 4. No response isolates the fault to the TCC solenoid or its wiring rather than internal converter wear

short_description: Torque Converter Clutch Circuit Performance/Stuck Off
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0741 (Torque Converter Clutch Circuit Performance/Stuck Off) sets when the TCM detects an open circuit, out-of-range current, or a stuck-on/stuck-off performance fault while driving the torque converter clutch solenoid that locks the converter for fuel economy at cruise. Causes are a failed TCC solenoid, contaminated fluid causing the solenoid or clutch plate to stick, or damaged control wiring.
fix_title: Test and replace TCC solenoid
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Test TCC solenoid resistance against spec • 2. Command the solenoid with a scan tool and verify a response • 3. Replace a solenoid that is open, shorted, or stuck • 4. Clear the code and verify lockup engages/releases smoothly

short_description: Torque Converter Clutch Circuit Performance/Stuck Off
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0741 (Torque Converter Clutch Circuit Performance/Stuck Off) sets when the TCM detects an open circuit, out-of-range current, or a stuck-on/stuck-off performance fault while driving the torque converter clutch solenoid that locks the converter for fuel economy at cruise. Causes are a failed TCC solenoid, contaminated fluid causing the solenoid or clutch plate to stick, or damaged control wiring.
fix_title: Repair TCC control circuit wiring
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Back-probe the driver circuit during a commanded test • 2. Voltage-drop test the harness back to the TCM • 3. Repair chafed or corroded conductors • 4. Confirm commanded lockup duty cycle is achieved

detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0743 (Torque Converter Clutch Circuit Electrical) sets when the TCM detects an open circuit, out-of-range current, or a stuck-on/stuck-off performance fault while driving the torque converter clutch solenoid that locks the converter for fuel economy at cruise. short_description: Shift Solenoid B
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0755 (Shift Solenoid B) sets when the TCM detects an open circuit, out-of-range current, or a stuck-on/stuck-off performance fault while driving one of the transmission's shift solenoids, which route hydraulic pressure to select the current gear. Every solenoid letter shares the same diagnosis path: causes are a failed solenoid coil, sludge/debris jamming the solenoid's spool, or damaged control wiring at the internal valve-body connector.
fix_title: Check transmission fluid level and filter condition
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Check fluid level and condition • 2. Change fluid and filter, since debris is the most common cause of a sticking shift solenoid • 3. Clear the code and drive-cycle through all gears • 4. Monitor for the code returning

short_description: Shift Solenoid B
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0755 (Shift Solenoid B) sets when the TCM detects an open circuit, out-of-range current, or a stuck-on/stuck-off performance fault while driving one of the transmission's shift solenoids, which route hydraulic pressure to select the current gear. Every solenoid letter shares the same diagnosis path: causes are a failed solenoid coil, sludge/debris jamming the solenoid's spool, or damaged control wiring at the internal valve-body connector.
fix_title: Command the named shift solenoid with a scan-tool actuator test
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Connect a scan tool with bi-directional solenoid control • 2. Command the named shift solenoid on and off • 3. Confirm line pressure or shift-quality PID responds to the command • 4. No response isolates the fault to that solenoid or its valve-body connector before condemning the whole pack

short_description: Shift Solenoid B
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0755 (Shift Solenoid B) sets when the TCM detects an open circuit, out-of-range current, or a stuck-on/stuck-off performance fault while driving one of the transmission's shift solenoids, which route hydraulic pressure to select the current gear. Every solenoid letter shares the same diagnosis path: causes are a failed solenoid coil, sludge/debris jamming the solenoid's spool, or damaged control wiring at the internal valve-body connector.
fix_title: Test and replace the named shift solenoid
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Test the named solenoid's resistance against spec • 2. Command it individually with a scan tool and listen/feel for a click and gear response • 3. Replace the solenoid, typically as part of the valve-body solenoid pack • 4. Clear the code and verify normal shifting

short_description: Shift Solenoid B
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P0755 (Shift Solenoid B) sets when the TCM detects an open circuit, out-of-range current, or a stuck-on/stuck-off performance fault while driving one of the transmission's shift solenoids, which route hydraulic pressure to select the current gear. Every solenoid letter shares the same diagnosis path: causes are a failed solenoid coil, sludge/debris jamming the solenoid's spool, or damaged control wiring at the internal valve-body connector.
fix_title: Repair shift solenoid wiring at the valve body connector
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Remove the valve body pan for access to the internal connector • 2. Inspect for corrosion or a broken pin at the solenoid connector • 3. Repair or replace the connector/harness section • 4. Refill fluid, clear the code, and verify commanded shifts

short_description: Particulate Filter Efficiency Below Threshold Bank 1
detailed_technical_explanation: P2002 (Particulate Filter Efficiency Below Threshold Bank 1) sets when the differential-pressure or soot-model calculation shows the diesel particulate filter is passing more particulate matter than its efficiency threshold allows, or when an abnormal pressure-drop signature indicates a cracked or missing filter substrate. A substrate cracked or melted by an uncontrolled regeneration event, a swapped-in filter that lacks the correct catalytic coating, and a leaking or misrouted differential-pressure sensor hose are the most common causes.
fix_title: Inspect DPF differential pressure sensor hoses for cracks or swapped routing
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Locate the two pressure sensor hoses running to ports ahead of and behind the DPF • 2. Check for melted, kinked, or disconnected hose sections along the exhaust heat shield • 3. Confirm the hoses are routed to the correct ports and not swapped • 4. Reseat the hoses, clear the code, and monitor for its return

short_description: Particulate Filter Efficiency Below Threshold Bank 1
detailed_technical_explanation: P2002 (Particulate Filter Efficiency Below Threshold Bank 1) sets when the differential-pressure or soot-model calculation shows the diesel particulate filter is passing more particulate matter than its efficiency threshold allows, or when an abnormal pressure-drop signature indicates a cracked or missing filter substrate. A substrate cracked or melted by an uncontrolled regeneration event, a swapped-in filter that lacks the correct catalytic coating, and a leaking or misrouted differential-pressure sensor hose are the most common causes.
fix_title: Perform a manual DPF regeneration and inspect soot loading
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Connect a scan tool and check the current soot load percentage and DPF status PIDs • 2. Drive at sustained highway speed or command a parked regeneration per the platform procedure • 3. Confirm exhaust back-pressure and soot load fall to the normal range afterward • 4. Recheck efficiency monitor completion

A substrate cracked or melted by an uncontrolled regeneration event, a swapped-in filter that lacks the correct catalytic coating, and a leaking or misrouted differential-pressure sensor hose are the most common causes.
fix_title: Send the DPF out for professional cleaning (ash removal)
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Remove the DPF assembly for inspection outside the vehicle • 2. Deliver it to a DPF cleaning service for pneumatic/thermal ash removal • 3. Weigh or pressure-test the cleaned filter against manufacturer specification before reinstalling • 4. Reinstall with new gaskets and clear the efficiency code

short_description: Particulate Filter Efficiency Below Threshold Bank 1
detailed_technical_explanation: P2002 (Particulate Filter Efficiency Below Threshold Bank 1) sets when the differential-pressure or soot-model calculation shows the diesel particulate filter is passing more particulate matter than its efficiency threshold allows, or when an abnormal pressure-drop signature indicates a cracked or missing filter substrate. A substrate cracked or melted by an uncontrolled regeneration event, a swapped-in filter that lacks the correct catalytic coating, and a leaking or misrouted differential-pressure sensor hose are the most common causes.
fix_title: Replace the diesel particulate filter assembly
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Confirm the filter substrate is physically cracked, melted, or missing via borescope or removal inspection • 2. Remove the DPF housing from the exhaust system, supporting adjacent flex joints • 3. Install the new filter with new gaskets and clamps, torquing to specification • 4. Clear codes and complete the DPF learn/reset procedure required by the platform

short_description: Post Catalyst Fuel Trim System Too Lean Bank 1
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P2096 (Post Catalyst Fuel Trim System Too Lean Bank 1) sets when the ECM must add more fuel than the calibrated limit allows to correct a lean condition on the indicated bank. The leading causes are a vacuum or intake air leak downstream of the MAF, a weak fuel pump or clogged filter, or a leaking exhaust manifold gasket confusing the upstream O2 sensor.
fix_title: Smoke-test intake for vacuum/air leaks
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Connect a smoke machine to the intake at idle • 2. Watch for smoke escaping at intake gaskets, PCV hoses, or brake booster line • 3. Replace any leaking gasket or hose • 4. Clear codes and verify fuel trims return toward 0%

short_description: Post Catalyst Fuel Trim System Too Lean Bank 1
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P2096 (Post Catalyst Fuel Trim System Too Lean Bank 1) sets when the ECM must add more fuel than the calibrated limit allows to correct a lean condition on the indicated bank. The leading causes are a vacuum or intake air leak downstream of the MAF, a weak fuel pump or clogged filter, or a leaking exhaust manifold gasket confusing the upstream O2 sensor.
fix_title: Replace fuel filter and test fuel pump output
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Replace the fuel filter if serviceable • 2. Measure fuel pump pressure and volume against spec under load • 3. Replace a weak pump if it fails the test • 4. Clear codes and confirm rail pressure holds steady

short_description: Post Catalyst Fuel Trim System Too Lean Bank 1
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P2096 (Post Catalyst Fuel Trim System Too Lean Bank 1) sets when the ECM must add more fuel than the calibrated limit allows to correct a lean condition on the indicated bank. fix_title: Inspect and repair exhaust manifold gasket leak
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Inspect the exhaust manifold gasket and studs near the upstream O2 sensor for leaks • 2. Replace the gasket and any corroded studs • 3. Torque to spec in sequence • 4. Clear codes and re-verify trim correction

short_description: Post Catalyst Fuel Trim System Too Rich Bank 1
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P2097 (Post Catalyst Fuel Trim System Too Rich Bank 1) sets when the ECM must remove more fuel than the calibrated limit allows to correct a rich condition on the indicated bank. Leading causes are a leaking fuel injector, a stuck-open fuel pressure regulator, a fouled MAF sensor overreporting airflow, or a failing fuel pressure sensor.
fix_title: Clean MAF sensor and inspect for false-high airflow readings
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Remove the MAF sensor and inspect the hot-wire element for contamination • 2. Clean with dedicated MAF cleaner (never touch the element) • 3. Reinstall and clear codes • 4. Compare live MAF g/s reading against expected values

short_description: Post Catalyst Fuel Trim System Too Rich Bank 1
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P2097 (Post Catalyst Fuel Trim System Too Rich Bank 1) sets when the ECM must remove more fuel than the calibrated limit allows to correct a rich condition on the indicated bank. Leading causes are a leaking fuel injector, a stuck-open fuel pressure regulator, a fouled MAF sensor overreporting airflow, or a failing fuel pressure sensor.
fix_title: Test fuel injectors for leakage and replace as needed
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Perform a fuel injector leak-down/drip test • 2. Identify any injector leaking past its seat • 3. Replace the faulty injector(s) and seals • 4. Clear codes and verify trims return toward 0%

short_description: Post Catalyst Fuel Trim System Too Rich Bank 1
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P2097 (Post Catalyst Fuel Trim System Too Rich Bank 1) sets when the ECM must remove more fuel than the calibrated limit allows to correct a rich condition on the indicated bank. fix_title: Replace stuck-open fuel pressure regulator
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Depressurize the fuel system • 2. Remove the regulator and inspect the diaphragm/valve for sticking • 3. Install the replacement regulator with new seals • 4. Clear codes and confirm rail pressure holds at commanded target

short_description: Throttle Actuator A Control Motor Circuit Range/Performance
detailed_technical_explanation: P2101 (Throttle Actuator A Control Motor Circuit Range/Performance) is set when the drive-by-wire throttle actuator motor's control circuit reports an open winding, a short, an out-of-range current draw, or the throttle plate's measured position that does not track the commanded motor current. Carbon and oil-vapor buildup on the throttle bore increasing motor load, a worn brush-type motor drawing excess current, and a corroded connector at the throttle body are the leading physical causes.
fix_title: Clean throttle bore and plate of carbon buildup
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Remove the intake ducting to access the throttle body plate • 2. Spray dedicated throttle body cleaner on the plate and bore with the plate held slightly open • 3. Wipe away loosened carbon with a lint-free cloth, avoiding the throttle position sensor end • 4. Reinstall ducting and perform a throttle relearn if the platform requires one

short_description: Throttle Actuator A Control Motor Circuit Range/Performance
detailed_technical_explanation: P2101 (Throttle Actuator A Control Motor Circuit Range/Performance) is set when the drive-by-wire throttle actuator motor's control circuit reports an open winding, a short, an out-of-range current draw, or the throttle plate's measured position that does not track the commanded motor current. Carbon and oil-vapor buildup on the throttle bore increasing motor load, a worn brush-type motor drawing excess current, and a corroded connector at the throttle body are the leading physical causes.
fix_title: Replace electronic throttle body/actuator motor assembly
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Disconnect the battery and unplug the throttle body electrical connector • 2. Remove the mounting bolts and lift off the throttle body with its gasket • 3. Install the replacement throttle body with a new gasket and torque bolts evenly • 4. Reconnect the battery and perform the required throttle/idle relearn procedure

short_description: Throttle Actuator A Control Motor Circuit Range/Performance
detailed_technical_explanation: P2101 (Throttle Actuator A Control Motor Circuit Range/Performance) is set when the drive-by-wire throttle actuator motor's control circuit reports an open winding, a short, an out-of-range current draw, or the throttle plate's measured position that does not track the commanded motor current. Carbon and oil-vapor buildup on the throttle bore increasing motor load, a worn brush-type motor drawing excess current, and a corroded connector at the throttle body are the leading physical causes.
fix_title: Repair throttle motor control circuit wiring
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Back-probe the throttle body connector and measure motor drive legs against the wiring diagram • 2. Trace the harness for a chafe point or corrosion between the throttle body and ECM • 3. Repair the damaged section with solder and heat-shrink • 4. Clear the code and confirm smooth throttle response through its full range

short_description: Throttle Actuator A Control Motor Circuit Range/Performance
detailed_technical_explanation: P2101 (Throttle Actuator A Control Motor Circuit Range/Performance) is set when the drive-by-wire throttle actuator motor's control circuit reports an open winding, a short, an out-of-range current draw, or the throttle plate's measured position that does not track the commanded motor current. Carbon and oil-vapor buildup on the throttle bore increasing motor load, a worn brush-type motor drawing excess current, and a corroded connector at the throttle body are the leading physical causes.
fix_title: Clean and reseat corroded throttle body connector
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Unplug the throttle body connector and inspect for corrosion or bent pins • 2. Clean contacts with electrical contact cleaner • 3. Apply dielectric grease and reseat the connector fully • 4. Clear the code and confirm it stays clear through a drive cycle

short_description: Throttle/Pedal Position Sensor/Switch A/B Voltage Correlation
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P2135 (Throttle/Pedal Position Sensor/Switch A/B Voltage Correlation) sets when the ECM detects an open, shorted, or out-of-range voltage, or an implausible rate of change, from a throttle body or accelerator pedal position sensor channel. Causes are a worn sensor track, a corroded connector, or chafed wiring between the sensor and the ECM.
fix_title: Inspect and clean throttle/pedal position sensor connector
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Locate the throttle body or accelerator pedal assembly connector • 2. Unplug and inspect for corrosion or bent pins • 3. Clean contacts and reseat firmly • 4. Clear codes and verify smooth voltage sweep

short_description: Throttle/Pedal Position Sensor/Switch A/B Voltage Correlation
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P2135 (Throttle/Pedal Position Sensor/Switch A/B Voltage Correlation) sets when the ECM detects an open, shorted, or out-of-range voltage, or an implausible rate of change, from a throttle body or accelerator pedal position sensor channel. Causes are a worn sensor track, a corroded connector, or chafed wiring between the sensor and the ECM.
fix_title: Replace throttle body or pedal position sensor assembly
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Unplug the connector and remove the mounting fasteners • 2. Install the replacement throttle body or pedal assembly • 3. Reconnect and perform a throttle relearn if required • 4. Clear codes and verify all channels track together

short_description: Throttle/Pedal Position Sensor/Switch A/B Voltage Correlation
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P2135 (Throttle/Pedal Position Sensor/Switch A/B Voltage Correlation) sets when the ECM detects an open, shorted, or out-of-range voltage, or an implausible rate of change, from a throttle body or accelerator pedal position sensor channel. Causes are a worn sensor track, a corroded connector, or chafed wiring between the sensor and the ECM.
fix_title: Repair throttle/pedal position sensor wiring
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Back-probe the reference, signal, and ground circuits at the connector • 2. Voltage-drop test each leg back to the ECM • 3. Repair chafed conductors with heat-shrink splices • 4. Confirm all sensor channels agree throughout the pedal/throttle sweep

short_description: Cooling System Performance
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P2181 (Cooling System Performance) sets when engine coolant fails to reach or maintain its regulating temperature within the expected time, or coolant flow/circulation is judged insufficient. Causes are a thermostat stuck open, a low coolant level, a failing water pump, or a chronically cold operating condition combined with a stuck-open thermostat.
fix_title: Check coolant level and inspect for external leaks
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Check coolant level cold and top off to spec • 2. Inspect hoses, radiator, and water pump weep hole for leaks • 3. Repair any obvious external leak source • 4. Clear codes and monitor warm-up time

short_description: Cooling System Performance
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P2181 (Cooling System Performance) sets when engine coolant fails to reach or maintain its regulating temperature within the expected time, or coolant flow/circulation is judged insufficient. Causes are a thermostat stuck open, a low coolant level, a failing water pump, or a chronically cold operating condition combined with a stuck-open thermostat.
fix_title: Replace stuck-open thermostat
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Drain coolant below the thermostat housing • 2. Remove the housing and replace the thermostat and gasket • 3. Refill coolant and bleed air from the system • 4. Confirm engine reaches operating temperature within expected time

short_description: Cooling System Performance
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P2181 (Cooling System Performance) sets when engine coolant fails to reach or maintain its regulating temperature within the expected time, or coolant flow/circulation is judged insufficient. fix_title: Replace failing water pump
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Drain coolant and remove drive components to access the water pump • 2. Remove the worn pump and inspect the impeller and bearing • 3. Install the replacement pump with a new gasket • 4. Refill, bleed, and verify stable circulation and temperature

short_description: O2 Sensor Signal Biased/Stuck Lean Bank 1 Sensor 1
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P2195 (O2 Sensor Signal Biased/Stuck Lean Bank 1 Sensor 1) sets when the ECM sees an open circuit, a stuck/biased voltage, or no signal activity from an oxygen sensor's signal circuit. Causes are a contaminated or worn-out sensor element, an exhaust leak near the sensor bung skewing readings, or damaged sensor wiring.
fix_title: Inspect for exhaust leaks near the O2 sensor
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Inspect the exhaust manifold and pipe joints near the sensor bung for leaks • 2. Listen and smoke-test for exhaust leaks upstream of the sensor • 3. Replace gaskets or tighten fasteners as needed • 4. Clear codes and monitor sensor voltage trace

short_description: O2 Sensor Signal Biased/Stuck Lean Bank 1 Sensor 1
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P2195 (O2 Sensor Signal Biased/Stuck Lean Bank 1 Sensor 1) sets when the ECM sees an open circuit, a stuck/biased voltage, or no signal activity from an oxygen sensor's signal circuit. Causes are a contaminated or worn-out sensor element, an exhaust leak near the sensor bung skewing readings, or damaged sensor wiring.
fix_title: Replace oxygen sensor
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Identify the affected bank/sensor position • 2. Remove the sensor with an O2 sensor socket • 3. Apply anti-seize to threads (avoid the tip) and install the new sensor • 4. Clear codes and confirm a normal switching voltage trace

short_description: O2 Sensor Signal Biased/Stuck Lean Bank 1 Sensor 1
detailed_technical_explanation: Code P2195 (O2 Sensor Signal Biased/Stuck Lean Bank 1 Sensor 1) sets when the ECM sees an open circuit, a stuck/biased voltage, or no signal activity from an oxygen sensor's signal circuit. fix_title: Repair O2 sensor signal circuit wiring
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Back-probe the signal and ground at the connector with the engine running • 2. Voltage-drop test the circuit back to the ECM • 3. Repair chafed or corroded wiring near the exhaust with high-temp splices • 4. Confirm proper switching activity

short_description: Lost Communication With ECM/PCM A
detailed_technical_explanation: U0100 sets when the gateway or another control module stops receiving any messages from a powertrain-side module or smart sensor -- the ECM/PCM, TCM, or one of the engine-bay LIN-networked sensors (MAP, MAF, EGR, turbo boost, fuel pressure, and similar) that report over a low-speed sub-bus feeding into the high-speed backbone. These nodes live in the engine compartment where connectors see more heat-cycling, vibration, and oil/coolant contamination than any other module class, so a burned or corroded engine-harness connector is the leading cause, followed by a blown fuse for that module's dedicated power circuit.
fix_title: Check the module's dedicated fuse and battery/ground connections
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Identify the fuse feeding the specific powertrain module or sensor node named in the code • 2. Inspect the fuse and the engine-bay ground strap for corrosion • 3. Replace the fuse if blown and retest for communication

short_description: Lost Communication With ECM/PCM A

fix_title: Inspect and clean the engine-harness connector at the named module
step_by_step_instructions: 1. Locate the connector for the module or sensor identified in the fault description • 2. Inspect for melted plastic, oil contamination, or backed-out terminals • 3. Clean contacts with electrical contact cleaner and apply dielectric grease on reassembly • 4. Clear codes and confirm the module rejoins the network

step_by_step_instructions: 1. Trace the sub-bus wire from the named module toward the gateway connection point • 2. Inspect for chafe against exhaust heat shields or engine mount brackets • 3. Cut out and splice in heat-resistant wire rated for engine-bay temperatures • 4. Protect the repair with high-temperature adhesive-lined heat-shrink • 5. Verify the module reports live data on a scan tool

step_by_step_instructions: 1. Confirm the module or smart sensor's internal transceiver has failed through isolation testing • 2. Remove and replace the component • 3. Program or configure the replacement using a factory-level scan tool where required • 4. Confirm stable communication and correct live data over a full drive cycle
